options.E: Branch by abstraction: Create an abstraction layer within the monolith. Swap implementations behind the abstraction from monolith code to calls to new microservices. Deploy microservices on ECS. Use shared Aurora cluster (same schema as Oracle) to avoid data synchronization complexity.

explanation: The Strangler Fig pattern with an anti-corruption layer is the proven approach for progressive monolith decomposition. API Gateway provides the routing layer to direct traffic to monolith or microservices. CDC via DMS keeps data synchronized without requiring dual-writes (which are error-prone). EventBridge enables loose coupling between new services. Each service gets an appropriate database (polyglot persistence). The anti-corruption layer prevents new services from being coupled to the monolith's data model. Option A mentions dual-write which is risky (inconsistency). Option B uses database views which create tight coupling. Option C is a big-bang rewrite (contradicts progressive). Option E shares a database which doesn't achieve true decomposition.

question:
A company runs a SaaS application serving 500 enterprise customers. Each customer has a dedicated Amazon RDS PostgreSQL instance (db.r5.large) for data isolation requirements. The total RDS spend is $380,000/month. Analysis shows: average CPU utilization across all instances is 15%, 80% of instances have peak usage only during their respective customer's business hours (varying time zones), and 50 instances belong to customers on the free tier (minimal usage). The company cannot consolidate databases due to contractual data isolation requirements.

Which approach achieves the GREATEST cost reduction while maintaining per-customer isolation?

options.A: Migrate all instances to Aurora Serverless v2 with minimum 0.5 ACU and maximum 8 ACU. Customers scale independently based on usage. Free-tier customers use minimum ACU during idle periods.
options.B: Right-size all instances to db.r5.medium (since average is 15% of r5.large). Purchase 1-year Reserved Instances for all 500 instances. Stop free-tier customer instances during off-hours.
options.C: Migrate to Aurora Serverless v2 with 0.5-16 ACU range. For free-tier customers, pause databases after 5 minutes of inactivity (Aurora Serverless v1 behavior). Purchase Aurora Reserved Instances for the baseline ACU across all instances.
options.D: Consolidate into multi-tenant Aurora clusters using PostgreSQL schemas for isolation (one schema per customer within shared clusters grouped by time zone). Use row-level security and separate IAM database authentication per customer.
options.E: Migrate to Aurora Provisioned with db.r6g.medium instances (Graviton, 20% cheaper). Purchase 3-year All Upfront Reserved Instances. Implement custom stop/start scheduling for free-tier instances using Lambda.

explanation: Aurora Serverless v2 with per-customer instances maintains contractual data isolation while dramatically reducing costs. At 15% average CPU utilization, most instances will run near their minimum ACU most of the time (0.5 ACU = ~$45/month vs db.r5.large = ~$760/month). During peak, instances scale up automatically. Free-tier customers remain at minimum ACU perpetually. The total cost reduction could exceed 80% for low-usage instances. Option B (right-size + RIs) still pays for 24/7 running instances with 15% utilization. Option C mentions Aurora Serverless v1 pause behavior which isn't available in v2 and you can't mix. Option D violates contractual isolation requirements. Option E is cheaper than current but still pays for always-on instances.

question:
A company's development team deploys 50 times per day across 20 microservices. Their current CI/CD pipeline uses CodePipeline with CodeBuild and deploys to ECS. Recently, they've had several production incidents caused by deployments: a configuration change that passed all tests but caused memory leaks under production load, an API change that broke a downstream service not covered by the service's tests, and a database migration that locked tables for 30 seconds causing timeouts.

Which combination of practices MOST effectively prevents deployment-caused incidents?

options.A: Implement contract testing between services using Pact. Add load testing in the pipeline using distributed load testing solution on AWS. Require database migration review as a pipeline gate. Implement feature flags for all changes.
options.B: Implement canary deployments with automated rollback based on error rates and latency percentiles. Add contract testing between services. Implement progressive database migrations (expand-contract pattern). Add load testing in a pre-production environment that mirrors production traffic patterns. Use AWS CodeDeploy deployment configuration with bake time.
options.C: Implement blue/green deployments with manual approval gates. Add end-to-end integration tests covering all service interactions. Require DBA approval for all database changes. Increase staging environment load testing.
options.D: Deploy to production with feature flags disabled. Monitor for 1 hour. Enable feature flags gradually. Implement chaos engineering to test resilience. Add contract tests. Use online schema migration tools.
options.E: Implement GitOps with ArgoCD for declarative deployments. Add Synthetic monitoring after each deployment. Implement service mesh for traffic management. Require pre-production sign-off from QA team.

explanation: This combination directly addresses each incident type: 1) Canary deployments + automated rollback would catch the memory leak — a small percentage of traffic hits the new version and metrics degradation triggers automatic rollback before full deployment. 2) Contract testing (Pact) catches API breaking changes by verifying consumer expectations against provider implementations — no need for full integration tests. 3) Expand-contract (progressive) database migrations eliminate table locks by separating schema changes from code changes (add new column, deploy code using both, migrate data, remove old column). 4) Load testing catches performance issues before production. Option A is similar but lacks canary deployments for safe production validation. Option C's manual gates don't scale for 50 deploys/day. Option D's 1-hour monitoring is too slow.

question:
A financial services company needs to implement a system that processes end-of-day batch reconciliation across 50 million transactions from multiple sources (trading systems, settlement systems, custodian records). The reconciliation involves complex matching logic (fuzzy matching on counterparty names, tolerance-based amount matching, cross-currency conversions). Unmatched items need investigation workflows. The entire batch must complete within a 4-hour overnight window. The system must maintain an audit trail of every matching decision.

Which architecture BEST handles this workload?

options.A: Step Functions orchestrating AWS Batch jobs. Each Batch job processes a partition of transactions using custom matching logic. DynamoDB for transaction state tracking. S3 for input/output files. Aurora PostgreSQL for the audit trail. Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow (MWAA) for scheduling.
options.B: AWS Glue ETL jobs for data preparation. Amazon EMR with Apache Spark for distributed matching logic. Results written to S3 in Parquet format. Athena for investigation queries. DynamoDB for workflow state. Step Functions for orchestration.
options.C: Lambda functions triggered by S3 uploads. Step Functions for matching workflow orchestration. DynamoDB for transaction pairs. RDS for audit trail. SQS for investigation queue. Quicksight for investigation dashboard.
options.D: Amazon EMR Serverless with Apache Spark for distributed reconciliation. S3 for data lake (input, output, audit trail in Parquet). AWS Glue Data Catalog for schema management. Step Functions for orchestration and error handling. Amazon MWAA for nightly scheduling. DynamoDB for investigation workflow state with TTL.
options.E: Amazon Redshift for loading all transaction data. Stored procedures for matching logic. Materialized views for investigation queries. S3 unload for audit archives. EventBridge Scheduler for nightly trigger.

explanation: EMR Serverless with Spark provides the distributed compute needed for 50M transaction matching within 4 hours without managing clusters (serverless scales automatically and shuts down after). Spark handles complex matching logic (fuzzy matching libraries, UDFs for tolerance/currency conversion) across massive datasets efficiently. S3 data lake in Parquet provides both performant processing and durable audit trail. Glue Data Catalog enables schema evolution. Step Functions handles orchestration, retry logic, and error paths. MWAA provides the scheduling. DynamoDB with TTL manages investigation workflows. Option A (Batch) works but is less efficient than Spark for this type of distributed data processing. Option B is similar but uses provisioned EMR (higher cost, cluster management). Option C (Lambda) can't handle 50M complex matching within time/memory limits. Option E (Redshift) could work but stored procedures are harder to maintain for complex matching logic.

question:
A global bank is implementing a landing zone on AWS with strict regulatory requirements. Different regulations apply in different regions: GDPR in Europe, SOX in the US, MAS-TCS in Singapore, and PCI-DSS globally for card processing. Each regulation has different requirements for data residency, encryption key management, access logging, and data retention. The bank has 300 AWS accounts and needs to ensure continuous compliance across all accounts while minimizing compliance team overhead.

Which governance architecture MOST effectively manages multi-regulatory compliance?

options.A: AWS Control Tower with custom guardrails per OU (EU, US, APAC OUs). AWS Config conformance packs for each regulation. AWS Audit Manager with custom frameworks combining relevant regulations per region. Security Hub for aggregated compliance scoring. Automated remediation via EventBridge and Systems Manager.
options.B: Deploy a GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) tool like ServiceNow on EC2. Integrate with AWS Config for technical controls. Manual quarterly audits per regulation. Custom dashboards in QuickSight for compliance visibility.
options.C: SCPs enforcing region restrictions per OU. AWS Config organization rules for each compliance requirement. Custom Lambda functions checking compliance daily. SNS alerts for violations. S3 bucket with compliance evidence for auditors.
options.D: AWS Audit Manager with pre-built frameworks (PCI-DSS, SOX). Custom frameworks for GDPR and MAS-TCS. Automated evidence collection from Config, CloudTrail, and Security Hub. Control Tower guardrails for preventive controls. Config conformance packs per OU with auto-remediation. Delegated administrator accounts per regulation for regional compliance teams.
options.E: Third-party compliance platform (Dome9/Prisma Cloud) for multi-framework assessment. Integration with AWS SSO for compliance team access. Automated reports per regulation. AWS Config as data source only.

explanation: This approach provides comprehensive multi-regulatory compliance: Audit Manager automates evidence collection (critical for reducing compliance team overhead), with pre-built frameworks for known standards and custom frameworks for others. Control Tower guardrails provide preventive compliance. Config conformance packs ensure detective controls with auto-remediation reducing manual effort. Delegated administrator accounts per regulation allow regional compliance teams to manage their regulatory requirements independently (separation of duties). Evidence is automatically collected from Config, CloudTrail, and Security Hub — reducing audit preparation from weeks to hours. Option A is close but lacks the structured evidence collection and delegation model. Option B introduces manual processes. Option C lacks the audit evidence automation. Option E relies on third-party tools when AWS-native tools cover the requirements.

question:
A media streaming company needs to build a content recommendation engine that serves personalized recommendations to 50 million users. The system must respond within 100ms at the API level. The recommendation model uses collaborative filtering trained on user viewing history (2 years, 10 billion events), real-time viewing sessions, and content metadata. The model needs daily retraining with the latest data. Recommendations must update in near-real-time when a user watches something new.

Which architecture provides personalized real-time recommendations at scale?

options.A: Amazon Personalize for the recommendation engine. Real-time events via Personalize PutEvents API. Personalize handles model training and serving. CloudFront for API caching (short TTL). DynamoDB for user profile enrichment.
options.B: Custom model training on SageMaker using large-scale matrix factorization. SageMaker real-time endpoint with auto-scaling for serving. Kinesis Data Streams for real-time event ingestion. Feature Store for online features (recent views). S3 for training data. EventBridge for daily retraining trigger.
options.C: Amazon Personalize for batch recommendations generated daily. ElastiCache Redis for serving pre-computed recommendations with sub-millisecond latency. Real-time personalization via a Lambda function that re-ranks Personalize results using the current session. Kinesis for session event collection.
options.D: SageMaker for model training on EMR-prepared features. Pre-compute recommendations for all users nightly and store in DynamoDB. Real-time re-ranking using a lightweight SageMaker endpoint that adjusts scores based on current session. Kinesis for event streaming to update session features in ElastiCache.
options.E: Apache Spark on EMR for model training using ALS (Alternating Least Squares). Store user-item matrices in S3. Serve recommendations via a custom Flask API on ECS. Update matrices hourly via EMR jobs. Redis for caching hot users.

explanation: At 50 million users, pre-computing recommendations nightly and storing in DynamoDB provides consistent sub-100ms response times regardless of traffic spikes (simple key-value lookup). The real-time re-ranking endpoint handles the near-real-time personalization requirement — it takes pre-computed base recommendations and adjusts them using the current session features stored in ElastiCache. This two-tier approach (batch base + real-time re-rank) is the industry-standard pattern for large-scale recommendation systems. Option A (Personalize) works for smaller scale but may not meet 100ms SLA at 50M users with complex models. Option B (real-time endpoint for all requests) is expensive at this scale and risks latency spikes. Option C is close but relies on Personalize batch which may not support the model complexity needed. Option E lacks the real-time personalization component.

question:
A company is migrating a large-scale VMware environment (2,000 VMs, 500 TB storage) to AWS. They have VMware vSphere 7 with vSAN. They want to maintain VMware operational patterns for 50% of the workloads (the operations team is VMware-certified but has limited AWS experience), while the other 50% should be migrated natively to EC2. The migration must not exceed 18 months. They also want to maintain the ability to move workloads between the VMware and native AWS environments as the team upskills.

Which strategy provides the BEST migration path with operational continuity?

options.A: VMware Cloud on AWS (VMC) for all 2,000 VMs initially. Use HCX for live migration with no downtime. Over 18 months, gradually migrate VMs from VMC to native EC2 using AWS Application Migration Service as the team gains AWS skills.
options.B: Deploy VMware Cloud on AWS for the 50% that needs VMware operations. Migrate these using HCX for live vMotion. Simultaneously migrate the other 50% to EC2 using AWS Application Migration Service (MGN). Both tracks run in parallel. Use VMware HCX for future workload mobility between VMC and EC2.
options.C: Migrate all VMs to EC2 using Application Migration Service. Provide VMware operations team with training on AWS. Accept 3-6 months of reduced operational efficiency during transition. Use CloudEndure for continuous replication.
options.D: Use AWS Outposts VMware variant for on-premises VMware compatibility. Migrate 50% to Outposts, 50% to EC2 in AWS regions. Maintain unified vSphere management across on-premises and Outposts.
options.E: Migrate 50% to EC2 using MGN. For the VMware-dependent workloads, deploy them on EC2 bare metal instances running VMware vSphere. Self-manage the VMware layer on EC2. Use VMFS datastores on EBS volumes.

explanation: VMware Cloud on AWS provides a fully managed VMware SDDC running on AWS infrastructure — the operations team uses familiar vSphere/vCenter tools while workloads run in AWS. HCX enables live migration (vMotion) without downtime. Running both tracks in parallel maximizes the 18-month timeline. HCX also supports workload mobility between VMC and native AWS, allowing future migration from VMC to EC2 as the team upskills. Option A migrates everything to VMC first which delays the EC2 migration track unnecessarily. Option C ignores the VMware expertise and creates operational risk. Option D (Outposts) keeps infrastructure on-premises which may not meet the migration goals. Option E (self-managed VMware on bare metal) adds significant operational burden without VMware Cloud on AWS benefits.

question:
A company operates a multi-player online game with 2 million concurrent users. They need to implement a chat system that supports: direct messages between players, group chat rooms (up to 100 players), guild channels (up to 5,000 members), global broadcast messages from game administrators, message history retrieval, and content moderation with profanity filtering and spam detection.

Which architecture supports these requirements at scale with the LOWEST operational overhead?

options.A: AWS AppSync with WebSocket subscriptions. DynamoDB for message storage. Lambda resolvers for message processing. Amazon Comprehend for content moderation. DynamoDB Streams triggering moderation Lambda.
options.B: Amazon IVS (Interactive Video Service) Chat for WebSocket management. Lambda for message processing and routing. DynamoDB for persistence. Amazon Comprehend custom classifier for moderation. API Gateway WebSocket as fallback.
options.C: Custom WebSocket servers on ECS with Redis pub/sub for message routing. DynamoDB for message persistence. ElastiCache Redis for presence and channel state. Custom profanity filter Lambda. Application Load Balancer with sticky sessions.
options.D: API Gateway WebSocket API with Lambda integration. DynamoDB for connections and message storage. Lambda for routing messages to appropriate connections. SNS for broadcast messages. Step Functions for moderation pipeline (Comprehend + custom rules).
options.E: Amazon ElastiCache for Redis with pub/sub for real-time messaging. API Gateway WebSocket for client connections. Lambda for connection management and message fan-out. DynamoDB for message history. Amazon Comprehend for moderation. CloudFront WebSocket support for global distribution.

explanation: API Gateway WebSocket API is fully managed and handles 2M concurrent connections without infrastructure management (lowest operational overhead). Lambda integration processes messages without managing servers. DynamoDB scales for message storage and connection tracking. SNS provides efficient broadcast to all subscribers (admin messages). Step Functions orchestrates the moderation pipeline cleanly. Option A (AppSync) has subscription limits that make 5,000-member guild channels challenging. Option B (IVS Chat) is designed for live streaming chat, not game messaging. Option C requires managing ECS clusters, Redis, and sticky sessions (high operational overhead). Option E uses Redis pub/sub which requires managing cluster scaling for 2M connections and doesn't natively handle the fan-out at guild scale without significant engineering.

question:
A company's data lake on S3 contains 2 PB of data. They run analytics using Amazon Athena (200 analysts running ad-hoc queries), Amazon EMR (nightly batch jobs processing 500 TB), and Amazon Redshift (BI dashboards querying summarized data). Monthly spend breakdown: S3 storage $45K, Athena queries $80K (scanning too much data), EMR $120K (r5.4xlarge cluster running 24/7), Redshift $60K (dc2.8xlarge 4-node cluster). Total: $305K/month.

Which optimization strategy achieves the GREATEST overall cost reduction?

options.A: Convert S3 data to Apache Parquet with partition by date and category. Implement Athena workgroups with query result reuse. Migrate EMR to EMR Serverless. Migrate Redshift to Redshift Serverless for variable BI usage.
options.B: Implement S3 Intelligent-Tiering for cold data. Create Athena views to limit scanned data. Right-size EMR to r5.2xlarge with auto-scaling and Spot instances. Add Redshift concurrency scaling for peak BI hours.
options.C: Convert data to Parquet/ORC with Snappy compression, partitioned by date/region/category. Create Athena partitioned tables with projection. Implement result caching. Replace 24/7 EMR with transient clusters (EMR on EKS or EMR Serverless) that spin up for nightly jobs only. Migrate Redshift to RA3 nodes with managed storage (data in S3, scale compute independently). Implement Redshift materialized views for dashboard queries.
options.D: Move all analytics to Redshift Spectrum (query S3 directly). Eliminate Athena. Use Redshift stored procedures for batch processing. Scale Redshift to 8 nodes for combined workload.
options.E: Implement Apache Iceberg tables on S3 for the data lake. Use Athena with Iceberg time-travel to reduce scans. Migrate EMR workloads to AWS Glue (serverless). Keep Redshift but add AQUA (Advanced Query Accelerator) for better performance.

explanation: This targets the largest cost drivers specifically: 1) Parquet + compression + partitioning reduces Athena data scanning by 90%+ (from $80K to ~$8K). 2) Partition projection eliminates S3 list operations (faster + cheaper). 3) Replacing 24/7 EMR with transient/serverless eliminates paying for idle compute (~70% savings if jobs only run 4 hours nightly). 4) RA3 nodes decouple storage from compute — same BI performance at lower cost (plus managed storage means only hot data is cached locally). 5) Materialized views reduce repetitive dashboard query compute. Combined savings: $80K→$8K (Athena) + $120K→$36K (EMR) + $60K→$40K (Redshift) = ~$176K savings (~58% reduction). Option A is similar but less specific about the key optimizations. Option B doesn't address the fundamental Athena scanning issue. Option D eliminates tool flexibility. Option E's Glue may cost more than transient EMR at 500 TB scale.

question:
A company has 500 Lambda functions across 20 microservices. They're experiencing issues with: cold starts causing timeout errors for synchronous APIs (p99 latency > 10 seconds), inconsistent error handling (some functions fail silently), memory-related throttling on high-throughput functions, and difficulty debugging issues across the distributed system. The team spends 30% of their time on operational issues rather than feature development.

Which combination of improvements MOST effectively reduces operational burden?

options.A: Enable Provisioned Concurrency for all API-triggered functions. Implement Lambda Powertools for structured logging, tracing, and metrics. Use CloudWatch Lambda Insights for memory/CPU profiling. Standardize error handling using Lambda Destinations for async functions and API Gateway error mapping for sync functions.
options.B: Migrate synchronous APIs from Lambda to ECS Fargate (eliminates cold starts). Keep async processing on Lambda. Implement X-Ray for distributed tracing. Use CloudWatch Logs Insights for debugging. Standardize using Lambda Layers for common code.
options.C: Implement Provisioned Concurrency for latency-sensitive functions based on usage patterns. Deploy Lambda Powertools (Python/TypeScript) across all functions for standardized observability (structured logs, correlation IDs, custom metrics). Use Power Tuning to right-size memory. Implement Lambda Destinations for async error routing to SQS dead-letter queues. Enable CloudWatch Lambda Insights. Create composite CloudWatch alarms for service-level health.
options.D: Increase Lambda timeout to maximum (15 minutes) for all functions. Increase memory to 3 GB for all functions. Enable active tracing with X-Ray. Set up CloudWatch alarms on each function's error metric. Create SNS notifications for all errors.
options.E: Migrate all Lambda functions to a single EKS cluster. Implement Istio service mesh for observability. Use Jaeger for distributed tracing. Implement circuit breakers at the mesh level. This eliminates Lambda-specific operational issues entirely.

explanation: This directly addresses each pain point: 1) Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold starts for latency-sensitive functions (targeted, not all 500). 2) Lambda Powertools provides structured logging with correlation IDs, custom metrics, and tracing annotations — standardized across all functions for consistent observability. 3) Power Tuning optimizes memory (reducing throttling and cost). 4) Lambda Destinations route async failures to DLQs systematically (no silent failures). 5) Lambda Insights provides function-level performance data. 6) Composite alarms reduce alert noise. Option A applies Provisioned Concurrency to all API functions which is expensive. Option B migrates away from Lambda entirely which is drastic. Option D throws resources at the problem without understanding root causes. Option E is a complete architecture rewrite.

question:
A company needs to implement a multi-region active-active architecture for their e-commerce platform. The platform handles 100,000 orders per day and requires that customers always see consistent product catalog data and their own order history. The system must survive a complete region failure with less than 1 minute of customer impact and zero data loss for completed orders.

Which architecture achieves active-active with zero data loss for orders?

options.A: DynamoDB global tables for product catalog and orders. API Gateway in each region with Route 53 latency-based routing. Lambda for business logic. SQS for order processing in each region. DynamoDB Streams for cross-region event propagation.
options.B: Aurora Global Database for orders (synchronous commit to secondary region using write forwarding with custom application logic verifying secondary commit). DynamoDB global tables for product catalog. CloudFront with origin failover. ECS services in both regions.
options.C: DynamoDB global tables for product catalog (eventual consistency acceptable). For orders: write to local DynamoDB table, synchronously write to SQS in both regions before confirming to customer, process orders from SQS. Route 53 health checks with failover. API Gateway + Lambda in each region.
options.D: Aurora Global Database for all data. Accept ~1 second RPO for orders during failover. Implement client-side retry logic to resubmit orders that may have been lost. Route 53 failover routing. Compensating transactions for duplicate detection.
options.E: Use a distributed consensus system (CockroachDB on EC2) spanning both regions for order data. DynamoDB global tables for catalog. Global Accelerator for traffic routing. The database handles multi-region consistency natively.

explanation: Zero data loss for orders requires synchronous confirmation from both regions before acknowledging the customer. Writing to SQS in both regions synchronously ensures the order exists in both regions before the customer receives confirmation. If one region fails, the other has the order in its SQS queue for processing. DynamoDB global tables handle the product catalog with eventual consistency (acceptable for catalog data). This pattern sacrifices a small amount of write latency (dual-region SQS write) for zero data loss guarantee. Option A (DynamoDB global tables for orders) has eventual consistency — potential data loss during failover. Option B (Aurora synchronous commit) isn't natively supported in Aurora Global Database. Option D explicitly accepts data loss. Option E adds operational complexity managing CockroachDB.

question:
A company is implementing least-privilege access across 100 AWS accounts for 500 developers organized in 20 teams. Each team owns 2-5 microservices. Developers need access to their team's resources in development accounts, limited read access in staging, and no direct access in production (deployment via CI/CD only). The company uses Azure AD for identity. Emergency break-glass access to production must be available but fully audited.

Which access management architecture BEST implements these requirements?

options.A: IAM Identity Center with Azure AD as IdP. Permission sets per environment tier: Dev (PowerUser for team's resources), Staging (ReadOnly), Prod (no regular access). Attribute-based access control (ABAC) using team tags to scope Dev access to team resources. Break-glass: separate permission set with MFA requirement and 1-hour session, triggering SNS notification to security team.
options.B: IAM Identity Center federated with Azure AD. Custom permission sets using ABAC with team cost-center tags. Session policies limiting Dev access to tagged resources. Staging read-only. Production access only via deployment roles assumed by CodePipeline. Break-glass via AWS SSO with temporary elevation using a custom approval workflow in Step Functions.
options.C: Cross-account IAM roles per team per environment. Azure AD SAML federation to a central identity account. Assume-role chain from identity account to target accounts. Session policies for resource scoping. Break-glass via root account credentials stored in Secrets Manager.
options.D: IAM Identity Center with Azure AD. All developers get ReadOnly base access everywhere. Use AWS CloudShell as the only access method. Permission boundaries prevent privilege escalation. Break-glass via support ticket to AWS.
options.E: Direct SAML federation from Azure AD to each account. IAM roles per team in each account. Custom identity broker Lambda for session management. Break-glass via separate federated role with longer session duration.

explanation: IAM Identity Center with Azure AD provides centralized identity management. ABAC using team tags (attached to resources and asserted in permission sets) enables fine-grained access without creating hundreds of static policies — when a resource is tagged with Team=X, only developers with the matching attribute can access it. Session policies add runtime restrictions. Production access exclusively via CI/CD roles (assumed by CodePipeline, not humans) enforces the no-direct-access requirement. The Step Functions approval workflow for break-glass provides automated audit trail, requires approval, and is time-limited. Option A is close but doesn't explicitly address the CI/CD-only production deployment model. Option C (assume-role chains) adds complexity and root credentials for break-glass is a security anti-pattern. Option D is too restrictive for development. Option E (per-account federation) doesn't scale.

question:
A company processes credit card payments and must maintain PCI-DSS compliance. They need to store card numbers for recurring billing while minimizing their PCI-DSS scope. The system processes 10 million transactions per day through their payment gateway. They also need to support tokenization for mobile wallets and provide fraud detection with sub-second scoring for each transaction.

Which architecture MINIMIZES PCI scope while meeting all requirements?

options.A: All payment processing in a dedicated VPC with no internet access. HSM (CloudHSM) for encryption key management. Card data in RDS encrypted at field level. Separate non-PCI VPC for business applications. VPC endpoint for communication between environments.
options.B: Use a third-party payment processor (Stripe/Braintree) for card storage and tokenization — completely out of PCI scope. Tokens stored in DynamoDB. Custom fraud detection model on SageMaker real-time endpoint called during transaction authorization. EventBridge for transaction event routing.
options.C: AWS Payment Cryptography for card data encryption and tokenization. Card vault in DynamoDB with field-level encryption using Payment Cryptography keys. Dedicated PCI-scoped VPC with WAF and Shield. SageMaker real-time endpoint for fraud scoring. Network segmentation via security groups and NACLs.
options.D: CloudHSM cluster for key management. Tokenization service on Lambda in isolated VPC. Token-to-PAN mapping in DynamoDB encrypted with HSM keys. Non-PCI workloads in separate accounts. Amazon Fraud Detector for ML-based fraud scoring. PrivateLink for cross-VPC communication.
options.E: Outsource card storage to a PCI-compliant token vault (TokenEx). Store tokens in Aurora. Build fraud detection using Amazon Fraud Detector trained on historical transaction data. API Gateway with mutual TLS for payment partner connectivity. Minimal PCI scope (only transit of card data).

explanation: Minimizing PCI scope means handling as little card data as possible. Using a PCI-compliant token vault (TokenEx, Basis Theory, etc.) means the company never stores card data — only tokens. PCI scope reduces to SAQ-A or SAQ-A-EP (only transit). Amazon Fraud Detector is purpose-built for payment fraud detection with sub-second inference (less operational overhead than custom SageMaker). Mutual TLS ensures secure partner connectivity. Option B (Stripe) eliminates the need for a payment gateway entirely but the question implies they operate their own gateway. Option C and D both keep card data in-scope (larger PCI footprint). Option A has the largest PCI scope (stores encrypted cards in RDS). The key insight is that outsourcing card storage to a certified vault provider is the most effective scope reduction strategy.

question:
A company is decomposing a monolithic application into microservices during migration to AWS. The monolith currently uses synchronous database transactions spanning multiple business domains (orders, inventory, payments) to maintain data consistency. For example, placing an order atomically decreases inventory and processes payment in a single database transaction. After decomposition, each domain will have its own database. The company cannot accept data inconsistency (e.g., order placed but inventory not decremented).

Which pattern BEST maintains data consistency across the decomposed microservices?

options.A: Implement distributed transactions using a two-phase commit (2PC) protocol across service databases. Use a custom transaction coordinator on ECS. Each service's database participates as a resource manager.
options.B: Implement the Saga pattern with orchestration. Step Functions orchestrates the order creation saga: create order → reserve inventory → process payment. Each step invokes a service and records the result. On failure, execute compensating transactions (cancel order, release inventory, reverse payment). Use DynamoDB for saga state.
options.C: Use Amazon Aurora with multiple schemas (one per domain) in a single cluster. Maintain cross-schema transactions using BEGIN/COMMIT. This preserves ACID while providing logical separation.
options.D: Implement event sourcing with CQRS. All state changes are events in Kinesis. Each service maintains its own read model from the event stream. Eventual consistency is acceptable because events are processed in order.
options.E: Use a shared DynamoDB table with transactions API. All services write to the same table in a single TransactWriteItems call. Use composite keys to separate domain data (PK: DOMAIN#entity_id).

explanation: The Saga pattern with orchestration (Step Functions) is the standard solution for maintaining data consistency across microservices without distributed transactions. Step Functions provides reliable orchestration with built-in retry, timeout, and error handling. Compensating transactions handle failures (e.g., if payment fails after inventory is reserved, Step Functions triggers inventory release). DynamoDB tracks saga state for recovery. The key insight: microservices with separate databases cannot use ACID transactions — instead, they achieve consistency through coordinated local transactions with compensation. Option A (2PC) is an anti-pattern in microservices (coordination overhead, availability reduction, doesn't work across heterogeneous databases). Option C defeats the purpose of decomposition. Option D accepts eventual consistency which violates the requirement. Option E uses a single database which defeats microservice independence.

question:
A company operates a data analytics platform that ingests 5 TB of data daily from 200 sources. They're experiencing data quality issues: duplicate records (causing inflated metrics), schema drift from upstream sources breaking pipelines, null values in required fields causing join failures, and stale data from sources that stop sending without notification. These issues result in incorrect business reports discovered days after the data was ingested.

Which data quality architecture MOST proactively detects and prevents these issues?

options.A: AWS Glue Data Quality rules applied to every Glue ETL job. Glue Data Catalog with schema registry for drift detection. CloudWatch metrics on data freshness. SNS alerts for quality failures. Data quarantine bucket for failed records.
options.B: Implement Great Expectations framework on EMR for data validation. Custom deduplication logic in Spark. Schema validation using Avro schema registry. Custom Lambda checking data freshness per source. Results published to a quality dashboard.
options.C: AWS Glue Data Quality with custom rulesets per source (uniqueness, completeness, freshness, referential integrity). Glue Schema Registry with compatibility modes (BACKWARD) to detect schema drift before it breaks pipelines. EventBridge Scheduler triggering freshness checks (alert if source hasn't sent data in expected interval). Glue Data Quality results published to CloudWatch for alerting. Failed records routed to a quarantine zone with automated reprocessing after investigation.
options.D: Amazon DataZone for data governance. Lake Formation with column-level statistics for quality metrics. Manual data steward reviews weekly. Athena queries to check for duplicates. Custom scripts for schema validation.
options.E: dbt (data build tool) running on ECS for data transformation with built-in tests (uniqueness, not_null, relationships). Custom source freshness tests. Slack alerts on test failures. Schema changes detected by comparing Glue Catalog snapshots daily.

explanation: This architecture addresses all four issues proactively: 1) Glue Data Quality rulesets enforce uniqueness (prevents duplicates), completeness (catches nulls in required fields), and referential integrity (validates joins will succeed). 2) Glue Schema Registry with BACKWARD compatibility mode rejects schema changes that break consumers — detecting drift before data enters the pipeline. 3) EventBridge Scheduler + freshness checks detect sources that stop sending (proactive vs discovering days later). 4) Quarantine zone prevents bad data from reaching reports while allowing investigation and reprocessing. CloudWatch integration enables alerting. Option A is similar but less specific about schema registry compatibility modes and freshness monitoring. Option B uses non-AWS tools adding operational overhead. Option D is too manual (weekly reviews). Option E (dbt) catches issues at transformation time but not at ingestion (too late — bad data already entered the lake).

question:
A government agency needs to deploy a classified workload on AWS that processes Secret-level data. The system must meet FedRAMP High and DoD IL5 requirements. The architecture must include end-to-end encryption, hardware-based key management, network isolation from the public internet, and FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules. The system also needs to integrate with the agency's existing on-premises SIPR network.

Which architecture meets all compliance requirements?

options.A: AWS GovCloud (US) region with dedicated VPC. CloudHSM for key management (FIPS 140-2 Level 3). AWS Direct Connect with MACsec encryption for SIPR connectivity. PrivateLink for all service access (no internet gateway). S3 with SSE-KMS using CloudHSM-backed keys. EC2 dedicated instances with encrypted EBS volumes.
options.B: Standard AWS commercial region with enhanced security configurations. Client-side encryption using customer-managed keys. VPN over the internet for SIPR connectivity. KMS with custom key store backed by CloudHSM. VPC with no internet gateway.
options.C: AWS GovCloud (US) with dedicated tenancy VPC. AWS KMS with FIPS 140-2 endpoints for key management. AWS Direct Connect with IPsec VPN overlay for SIPR connectivity. All traffic through AWS Network Firewall. NitroTPM-enabled EC2 instances. S3 with SSE-KMS. Systems Manager for patch management without internet access.
options.D: AWS Secret Region (SC2S or C2S) for classified workloads. Dedicated AWS infrastructure operated by cleared personnel. Physical network isolation. CloudHSM for key management. Direct connection to SIPR via government-controlled network.
options.E: AWS Outposts installed in the agency's SCIF (Sensitive Compartmented Information Facility). Workloads run on Outposts with no connectivity to AWS Region. CloudHSM on-premises for key management. Air-gapped from internet.

explanation: For Secret-level classified data at IL5, AWS Secret Region (formerly SC2S - Secret Commercial Cloud Services) is specifically designed and accredited for classified workloads up to Secret. It provides physically isolated infrastructure operated by cleared personnel, direct SIPR connectivity, and meets all DoD IL5 requirements by design. Option A (GovCloud) is FedRAMP High but only supports up to IL5 for some use cases — Secret-level data processing requires the Secret Region. Option B (commercial region) cannot handle classified data regardless of security configurations. Option C (GovCloud) is close but GovCloud's authorization level may not cover Secret data depending on the specific workload classification. Option E (Outposts) with no region connectivity loses most AWS service capabilities.

question:
A company operates a machine learning platform where data scientists train models using SageMaker. Monthly spend is $500,000: 60% on training jobs (mix of single-GPU and multi-GPU distributed training, jobs lasting 2-48 hours), 25% on notebooks (100 data scientists with ml.m5.xlarge instances running during work hours), 10% on real-time inference endpoints (20 models in production), and 5% on data processing. Training jobs are experimental — 40% are cancelled within the first hour when scientists see poor initial metrics.

Which optimization strategy provides the GREATEST cost reduction?

options.A: Use SageMaker Managed Spot Training for all jobs. Implement SageMaker Savings Plans for notebooks. Use multi-model endpoints to consolidate inference. Implement lifecycle configurations to stop idle notebooks.
options.B: Spot Training for long-running jobs (>4 hours) with checkpointing. SageMaker Savings Plans covering baseline notebook and inference spend. Multi-model endpoints for inference consolidation. Auto-scaling on endpoints. Lifecycle configurations stopping notebooks after 1 hour of inactivity. Implement early stopping in training scripts (automatic termination when metrics plateau).
options.C: Migrate training to EC2 Spot instances for more instance type flexibility. Use JupyterHub on EKS instead of SageMaker notebooks. Deploy inference on ECS with Spot. Accept some operational overhead for 50% cost savings.
options.D: Purchase ml.p3.2xlarge Reserved capacity for training. Convert all notebooks to SageMaker Studio with shared compute. Use SageMaker Serverless Inference for low-traffic models. Enable S3 Express One Zone for training data.
options.E: Implement training job spot instances for all jobs. Use SageMaker Studio notebooks with auto-shutdown. Deploy inference on AWS Inferentia chips (Inf1 instances) for 70% inference cost reduction. Implement SageMaker Experiments for tracking and early termination of poor runs.

explanation: This targets the largest cost drivers with appropriate strategies: 1) Spot Training for long jobs saves 60-90% on the 60% training spend — but only jobs >4 hours (checkpointing overhead isn't worthwhile for short jobs, and 40% are cancelled quickly anyway). 2) Early stopping automatically terminates the 40% of jobs with poor metrics, saving wasted compute. 3) Savings Plans cover the predictable baseline (notebooks during work hours + inference endpoints). 4) Multi-model endpoints reduce inference costs by sharing infrastructure. 5) Auto-scaling handles variable inference traffic. 6) Lifecycle configurations eliminate idle notebook costs. Combined savings: ~50-60% of training (Spot + early stopping) + ~40% on notebooks + ~30% on inference = ~$200K+ monthly savings. Option A applies Spot to all jobs including short ones (checkpoint overhead issue). Option C trades managed service benefits for marginal savings. Option D (Reserved) is inflexible for experimental workloads.

question:
A company with 200 AWS accounts is implementing a centralized networking model. They need to provide internet access, DNS resolution, and VPN connectivity as shared services. Currently, each account has its own NAT Gateway ($0.045/GB + $0.045/hr per gateway = ~$3,000/month per account for typical usage), its own Route 53 Resolver endpoints, and individual site-to-site VPN connections. The total networking overhead across all accounts is $600,000/month.

Which centralized architecture MOST reduces cost while maintaining isolation and scalability?

options.A: Transit Gateway with a centralized egress VPC containing NAT Gateways. All account VPCs route internet traffic through Transit Gateway to the egress VPC. Centralized Route 53 Resolver endpoints in a shared services VPC with RAM-shared resolver rules. Single Transit Gateway VPN attachment replacing per-account VPNs.
options.B: AWS PrivateLink for all inter-service communication (eliminating most internet egress). Centralized proxy fleet (Squid on EC2) in a shared VPC for required internet access. Keep per-account DNS but implement Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall centrally.
options.C: Transit Gateway with centralized egress VPC using NAT Gateways sized for aggregate traffic. Centralized AWS Network Firewall for inspection and filtering. Route 53 Resolver endpoints in shared services VPC with RAM-shared rules and query logging. Transit Gateway site-to-site VPN attachments (2 for redundancy) replacing 200 individual VPNs. VPC endpoint policies for service-level isolation.
options.D: Keep NAT Gateways per account for isolation but use smaller accounts. Consolidate VPN to AWS Client VPN. Implement Route 53 profiles for centralized DNS. Use AWS Cloud WAN for simplified routing.
options.E: Replace NAT Gateways with NAT instances (t3.micro) in each account for cost savings. Centralize VPN through a single Direct Connect connection. Keep Route 53 Resolver per account but add conditional forwarding.

explanation: Centralized egress is the biggest cost saver: replacing 200 NAT Gateways ($600K+) with a few in a centralized VPC dramatically reduces the per-gateway hourly charges (from 200× $32/month to perhaps 6× $32/month for high-availability pair in 3 AZs). Data processing charges remain but hourly charges drop by ~97%. Network Firewall in the egress VPC adds security inspection centrally. Centralized Route 53 Resolver eliminates 200 sets of endpoints ($0.125/hr each = $36K/month for 200 accounts). Replacing 200 VPN connections with 2 Transit Gateway VPN attachments saves $72K/month in VPN charges. VPC endpoint policies maintain per-account isolation despite shared egress. Option A is similar but lacks Network Firewall and doesn't mention VPC endpoint policies for isolation. Option D keeps per-account NAT (no savings). Option E (NAT instances) introduces operational risk and single points of failure.

question:
A company needs to build an event-driven architecture that processes 1 million events per second with exactly-once processing semantics. Events must be processed in order within each customer partition (100,000 customers). Processing involves enrichment from a reference database, applying business rules, and writing results to multiple downstream systems (data warehouse, notification service, audit log). If any downstream write fails, the entire event must be retried without producing duplicates in systems that already succeeded.

Which architecture provides exactly-once semantics with ordered processing?

options.A: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with 1000 shards (customer ID as partition key). Lambda with event source mapping (parallelization factor 1 for ordering). DynamoDB for idempotency tokens. Step Functions for orchestrating downstream writes with compensation on failure.
options.B: Amazon MSK (Kafka) with transactions enabled. Kafka exactly-once semantics (EOS) for read-process-write within Kafka. Kafka Connect sinks with idempotent producers for downstream systems. Consumer groups with one partition per customer grouping for ordering.
options.C: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with enhanced fan-out. Kinesis Client Library (KCL) on ECS for ordered processing per shard. DynamoDB conditional writes for deduplication. SQS FIFO queues for downstream delivery with message deduplication IDs. Dead-letter queues for failures with replay capability.
options.D: Amazon SQS FIFO queues with message group IDs per customer. Lambda consumers with reserved concurrency. DynamoDB transactions for atomic multi-table writes. Idempotency keys in every operation.
options.E: Amazon EventBridge with custom event bus. Event archive for replay. Lambda with Destinations for success/failure routing. DynamoDB for state management. Step Functions for downstream orchestration.

explanation: This architecture achieves effectively-once processing: Kinesis with customer ID as partition key ensures ordering within a customer. KCL on ECS provides checkpointing and ordered processing per shard. DynamoDB conditional writes (PutItem with condition attribute_not_exists) provide deduplication — if a retry attempts to process an already-processed event, the conditional write fails harmlessly. SQS FIFO queues with deduplication IDs for downstream delivery prevent duplicate notifications/audit entries. If a downstream write fails, the checkpoint isn't advanced, triggering reprocessing — but deduplication ensures previously-successful writes aren't duplicated. Option A (Lambda) has concurrency limits at 1M events/sec. Option B (Kafka EOS) provides exactly-once within Kafka but not for external systems — sinks still need idempotency. Option D (SQS FIFO) has a 70,000 TPS limit per queue. Option E (EventBridge) has throughput limits and doesn't guarantee ordering.

question:
A company is migrating their self-managed Kubernetes clusters (15 clusters, 3,000 pods across 500 nodes) to Amazon EKS. They use custom admission controllers, Istio service mesh, custom CSI drivers for their SAN storage, and proprietary monitoring tools integrated with their on-premises Prometheus/Grafana stack. Developers deploy via ArgoCD and Helm charts. The migration must not disrupt the 50 development teams' workflows.

Which migration approach MINIMIZES disruption to development teams?

options.A: Create EKS clusters and migrate workloads using Velero (backup from self-managed, restore to EKS). Replace custom CSI with EBS CSI driver. Replace Istio with AWS App Mesh. Replace Prometheus with CloudWatch Container Insights. Retrain teams on new tools.
options.B: Deploy EKS clusters matching existing cluster configurations. Migrate custom admission controllers to EKS via webhooks. Keep Istio service mesh on EKS (supported). Replace custom CSI with EFS CSI driver for shared storage and EBS CSI for block. Point ArgoCD to new EKS clusters. Maintain Prometheus (Amazon Managed Prometheus) and Grafana (Amazon Managed Grafana) — same interfaces, managed backends. Migrate team by team with canary traffic shifting.
options.C: Use EKS Anywhere on-premises first to standardize on EKS APIs. Then migrate EKS Anywhere clusters to EKS in AWS using cluster-to-cluster migration. Maintain all existing tooling during the intermediate step.
options.D: Create one large EKS cluster to replace all 15 clusters. Use namespaces for isolation. Migrate all Helm charts simultaneously. Replace all custom tooling with AWS-native equivalents.
options.E: Run self-managed Kubernetes on EC2 (identical to on-premises setup). This preserves everything exactly. Plan a future phase to convert to managed EKS.

explanation: This approach preserves development team workflows: ArgoCD continues working (just points to new clusters). Helm charts don't change. Istio runs on EKS without modification. Amazon Managed Prometheus/Grafana provides the same query interface (PromQL) and dashboards teams already use — just managed. Custom admission controllers work via webhooks on EKS. EFS/EBS CSI drivers replace custom SAN drivers with standard Kubernetes storage interfaces. Team-by-team migration with canary reduces risk. Developers see minimal change in their day-to-day tools. Option A replaces too many tools simultaneously (major disruption). Option C adds an unnecessary intermediate step. Option D (single cluster) changes isolation model. Option E doesn't actually migrate to EKS.

question:
A company has implemented AWS Well-Architected best practices but is struggling with cost anomalies — unexpected spikes in their $2M monthly bill. Recent anomalies include: a Lambda function with infinite recursion costing $50K overnight, a forgotten EMR cluster running for 3 weeks after a test ($30K), a DynamoDB table auto-scaled to maximum and never scaled down ($20K), and data transfer costs from a misconfigured S3 replication rule ($15K). They need to prevent these categories of cost anomalies.

Which combination provides the MOST effective cost anomaly prevention and rapid response?

options.A: AWS Budgets with threshold alerts at 80%, 100%, 120% of expected spend. AWS Cost Anomaly Detection for ML-based detection. Budget actions to apply SCPs when budgets are breached. Tag-based budgets per team for accountability.
options.B: AWS Cost Anomaly Detection with monitors per service and per team (linked accounts). SNS + Lambda integration for automated response to anomalies (e.g., throttle Lambda concurrency, terminate untagged EMR clusters older than 24 hours). AWS Budgets with actions that switch IAM policies to deny new resource creation when budget exceeds 110%. Mandatory resource tagging (SCP-enforced) with cost-center and TTL tags. EventBridge rules triggering cleanup Lambda for resources exceeding their TTL tag.
options.C: Third-party cost management tool (CloudHealth) for anomaly detection. Slack alerts for the finance team. Monthly cost reviews with engineering leads. Implement approval workflows for resources exceeding $1,000/month.
options.D: AWS Cost Explorer daily reports to management. Trusted Advisor cost optimization checks. AWS Config rules checking for idle resources. Manual cleanup sprints quarterly.
options.E: Implement AWS Organizations with per-account spending limits (SCPs denying all actions when limit reached). Use reserved capacity for all services to cap maximum spend. Require pre-approval for any new service deployment.

explanation: This provides multiple layers of defense: 1) Cost Anomaly Detection's ML catches unusual patterns (like sudden Lambda spikes or unexpected EMR costs) faster than static budget thresholds. 2) Automated response via Lambda can immediately throttle or terminate offending resources (preventing the Lambda recursion from running all night). 3) Budget actions restrict new resource creation when overspending (preventing further damage). 4) TTL tags with automated cleanup prevent forgotten resources (EMR test cluster would be terminated after its TTL expires). 5) Mandatory tagging enables accountability and ensures cleanup automation can identify resource owners. Option A has alerts but no automated remediation for the fastest-moving anomalies. Option C relies on human review (too slow for overnight Lambda recursion). Option D is entirely reactive. Option E is too restrictive for normal operations.

question:
A company is building a serverless data pipeline that must process exactly 10 TB of data daily from 50 different source systems. Data arrives throughout the day in various formats (JSON, CSV, Parquet, XML). The pipeline must: validate schema and data quality, standardize to a common format (Parquet with Iceberg tables), deduplicate based on business keys, and make data available for querying within 30 minutes of arrival. The company has 3 data engineers and wants to minimize operational overhead.

Which architecture BEST balances the 30-minute SLA with minimal operations for a small team?

options.A: S3 for raw landing. AWS Glue crawlers for schema discovery. Glue ETL jobs (Spark) for transformation and deduplication. Glue Data Catalog for Iceberg table management. Glue Data Quality for validation. EventBridge triggering jobs on S3 arrival. Athena for querying.
options.B: S3 for raw landing. Lambda triggered on upload for format validation. Step Functions orchestrating Glue ETL jobs for each source. Glue jobs transform to Parquet and write to Iceberg tables. Glue bookmarks for incremental processing. Athena with Iceberg for querying. CloudWatch for monitoring all pipeline stages.
options.C: S3 for raw landing. AWS Glue with auto-generated ETL scripts for each source format. Glue Data Quality rulesets for validation. Glue ETL jobs writing to S3 with Iceberg table format (managed via Glue Data Catalog). S3 event notifications → EventBridge → Glue workflow per source (schema validation → quality check → transform → deduplicate → write Iceberg). Athena for query. Glue job monitoring via CloudWatch.
options.D: Kinesis Data Firehose for ingestion from all sources. Lambda for format transformation. Firehose delivers Parquet to S3. Glue Crawler updates catalog. Athena for querying. Custom deduplication via Athena CTAS queries.
options.E: Amazon MSK for ingestion. Apache Flink on Kinesis Data Analytics for real-time transformation. Flink writes to Apache Iceberg tables on S3. Flink handles deduplication with keyed state. Athena for querying.

explanation: For a 3-person team processing 10 TB daily from 50 sources with a 30-minute SLA, Glue provides the most operationally efficient solution: serverless (no infrastructure management), auto-generated ETL scripts (less code to maintain per source), built-in data quality rules, native Iceberg support via Data Catalog, and event-driven execution meeting the 30-minute SLA. Glue workflows provide per-source orchestration with dependency management. Option A uses crawlers which add latency and aren't needed if schemas are known. Option B (Step Functions + Glue) adds orchestration complexity. Option D (Firehose) doesn't support complex deduplication or Iceberg natively. Option E (MSK + Flink) is powerful but requires significant operational expertise — too complex for 3 engineers to maintain reliably.

question:
A multinational company needs to implement data sovereignty controls ensuring that customer data processed in specific countries never leaves those countries' AWS regions, even for backup, logging, or operational purposes. This applies to 15 countries with varying data residency laws. Some AWS services (like CloudTrail, Config) have control plane operations that route through us-east-1. The company uses 80 AWS accounts.

Which approach MOST comprehensively enforces data sovereignty?

options.A: SCPs denying all actions outside the designated region per country OU. VPC endpoints for all services to keep traffic within the region. S3 bucket policies denying cross-region operations. Disable S3 Transfer Acceleration and cross-region replication.
options.B: AWS Control Tower with region-deny guardrails customized per OU. Data Perimeter policy using resource-level conditions for region restriction. VPC endpoints with endpoint policies restricting to local-region resources only.
options.C: SCPs with region restriction (aws:RequestedRegion condition) per country OU, with explicit exceptions for global services (IAM, Route 53, CloudFront origin in correct region). Regional CloudTrail trails (not organization trail) writing to same-region S3 buckets. VPC endpoints for all data-plane operations. S3 bucket policies with aws:SourceVpc conditions. KMS keys restricted to the local region with key policy preventing cross-region grants. Disable account-level S3 public access and cross-region features. Regular compliance audits via Config rules checking for cross-region resources.
options.D: AWS Dedicated Local Zones in each country for guaranteed physical data residency. All workloads run in Local Zones. No connectivity to parent region.
options.E: Use third-party data sovereignty tools (Virtru, Thales) for encryption. Customer-managed keys that physically cannot be exported. Even if data transits other regions, it's encrypted with country-specific keys.

explanation: Comprehensive data sovereignty requires multiple layers: SCPs enforce that no resources can be created outside the designated region. Exceptions for global services (IAM, Organizations, Route 53) are necessary because they only have endpoints in specific regions. Regional CloudTrail trails (not organization trails) ensure log data stays in-region. VPC endpoints keep data-plane traffic within the region. S3 bucket policies with VPC conditions prevent data access from outside the local network. KMS regional restrictions ensure encryption keys can't be used cross-region. Config rules provide continuous compliance monitoring. Option A doesn't address CloudTrail or KMS cross-region concerns. Option B (Control Tower region-deny) is coarser and doesn't address all the edge cases. Option D (Local Zones) has very limited service availability. Option E (encryption only) doesn't prevent data from physically leaving the region.

question:
A company needs to implement a blue/green deployment strategy for their entire application stack which includes: 30 microservices on ECS, 5 Aurora clusters, 3 ElastiCache Redis clusters, 2 OpenSearch domains, and 15 DynamoDB tables. They deploy weekly and need the ability to instantly rollback the entire stack (all services and databases) to the previous version if issues are detected within 1 hour of deployment.

Which approach enables full-stack instant rollback?

options.A: Maintain two complete environments (blue/green). Route 53 weighted routing switches traffic. Both environments share databases (only application layer is blue/green). Database changes use backward-compatible migrations only.
options.B: ECS blue/green via CodeDeploy for services. Aurora cloning for database blue/green (create clone before deployment, switch endpoint if rollback needed). ElastiCache snapshots before deployment. OpenSearch blue/green index aliases. DynamoDB point-in-time recovery for rollback.
options.C: Full infrastructure duplication using CloudFormation StackSets. Two complete stacks (blue/green) including all databases. Global Accelerator for traffic switching. Database synchronization via DMS during the blue/green period. On rollback, switch traffic back and DMS reverse-replicates any new data.
options.D: ECS blue/green via CodeDeploy with weighted target groups. Database schema changes deployed separately using expand-contract pattern (always backward compatible). ElastiCache and OpenSearch maintained as shared services (no rollback needed — application handles version differences). DynamoDB tables shared with application-level versioning. Rollback only requires switching ECS task definitions.
options.E: Deploy new version alongside old version. Use feature flags to gradually enable new functionality. Disable flags to rollback. No infrastructure changes needed. Databases always maintain backward compatibility.

explanation: The key insight is that database rollback is extremely complex and risky (data loss potential). The industry best practice is to make database changes backward-compatible using the expand-contract pattern: 1) Expand — add new columns/tables (old code still works). 2) Deploy new code. 3) Contract — remove old columns in a future release. This means the 'old' ECS services can always work with the 'current' database state. Rollback is then simply an ECS task definition switch (instant via CodeDeploy). ElastiCache and OpenSearch are treated as shared infrastructure that serves both versions. Option A acknowledges shared databases but doesn't explain the compatibility strategy. Option B (Aurora cloning) doesn't handle new data written after deployment. Option C (full duplication with DMS) is extremely expensive and DMS reverse-replication is unreliable for rollback. Option E (feature flags) requires application changes for every deployment.

question:
A company runs a large-scale API platform serving 500 million API calls per day. Their current architecture uses API Gateway (REST API) → Lambda → DynamoDB. Monthly costs: API Gateway $175K (primarily request charges at $3.50/million), Lambda $80K, DynamoDB $45K. The APIs are a mix of public-facing (30%) and internal service-to-service (70%). Response payloads average 2 KB.

Which optimization MOST reduces total cost while maintaining functionality?

options.A: Migrate all APIs to API Gateway HTTP APIs ($1.00/million requests). Enable API caching for public APIs. Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency to reduce duration-based costs.
options.B: Replace API Gateway with Application Load Balancer ($0.008/LCU-hour) for internal APIs. Keep API Gateway HTTP APIs for public APIs (authentication, throttling, usage plans). Migrate Lambda to ECS Fargate for high-throughput internal services. Keep DynamoDB with DAX caching.
options.C: Implement CloudFront in front of API Gateway for public APIs (cached responses reduce origin calls). Migrate to HTTP APIs. Use Lambda response streaming to reduce payload costs. Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling with reserved capacity.
options.D: Replace everything with ECS Fargate behind ALB. Self-manage routing, authentication, and throttling. Use ElastiCache instead of DynamoDB for hot data. Accept increased operational complexity for cost savings.
options.E: Migrate to API Gateway HTTP APIs for all traffic. Enable API Gateway caching (0.5 GB) for all endpoints. Use Lambda Power Tuning to optimize all functions. Purchase DynamoDB Reserved Capacity for predictable tables.

explanation: The biggest cost driver is API Gateway at $175K for 500M calls/day (~15B/month). The key optimization is recognizing that 70% of traffic is internal service-to-service which doesn't need API Gateway features (authentication, throttling, usage plans, developer portal). ALB handles routing at a fraction of the cost — an ALB processing 350M internal calls/day costs roughly $2-3K/month vs $122K on API Gateway. Public APIs move to HTTP APIs ($1/million vs $3.50/million, saving ~60%). This alone saves ~$130K/month. Migrating high-throughput Lambda to Fargate reduces per-request overhead for stable traffic. Option A (HTTP APIs only) saves on API Gateway but still overpays for internal traffic ($1/M × 10.5B internal calls = $10.5K vs ALB at $2-3K). Option C (CloudFront caching) helps for cacheable APIs but many API calls are unique. Option D replaces too aggressively. Option E still routes all traffic through API Gateway.

question:
A company is migrating their Windows-based application estate (200 servers running .NET Framework 4.x applications on Windows Server 2016/2019, with SQL Server 2016/2019 Standard and Enterprise editions) to AWS. The estate includes: 50 IIS web servers, 80 application servers running Windows services, 40 SQL Server instances (mix of Standard and Enterprise), and 30 file servers with 100 TB of data accessed via SMB shares. They want to maximize cost savings, especially on SQL Server licensing.

Which migration approach MAXIMIZES cost savings on Microsoft licensing?

options.A: Rehost all servers on EC2 with License Mobility. Use BYOL for SQL Server Enterprise (dedicated hosts for license compliance). Migrate file servers to FSx for Windows File Server. Keep .NET Framework applications as-is.
options.B: Migrate web servers to Elastic Beanstalk running .NET on Windows. Migrate SQL Server Standard to RDS for SQL Server. Migrate SQL Server Enterprise to RDS Custom for SQL Server (reduces license cost vs EC2). File servers to FSx for Windows. Application servers rehosted on EC2.
options.C: Replatform .NET Framework to .NET 6 on Linux (eliminates Windows Server licensing). Migrate SQL Server Standard to Aurora PostgreSQL (eliminates SQL licensing). Migrate SQL Server Enterprise to Babelfish for Aurora PostgreSQL. File servers to EFS. Use AWS Graviton instances for additional savings.
options.D: Rehost on EC2 with AWS-provided Windows Server licenses (License Included). Migrate SQL Server Standard to RDS for SQL Server (License Included — no separate SQL license needed). For SQL Server Enterprise: migrate to EC2 Dedicated Hosts with BYOL (existing Enterprise licenses). Consolidate file servers to FSx for Windows. Over time, replatform .NET applications to .NET 6 on Linux containers.
options.E: Migrate everything to Azure for Microsoft licensing benefits (Azure Hybrid Benefit). Use Azure SQL Managed Instance. Keep Windows workloads on Azure where Microsoft licensing is cheapest.

explanation: This approach optimizes licensing at each tier: 1) RDS for SQL Server (License Included) eliminates SQL Server Standard license cost — AWS bundles it into the hourly rate which is often cheaper than BYOL for Standard edition. 2) EC2 Dedicated Hosts for SQL Server Enterprise allows BYOL — reusing existing Enterprise licenses (most expensive licenses) saves the most money. 3) AWS-provided Windows Server licenses (included in EC2 hourly rate) avoid separate Windows Server licensing management. 4) FSx for Windows replaces 30 file servers with a managed service (eliminates 30 Windows licenses). 5) Future replatform to .NET 6 on Linux eliminates Windows Server costs entirely. Option A uses BYOL everywhere which isn't optimal for Standard edition. Option B (RDS Custom) is for Enterprise edge cases, not the primary choice. Option C (full Linux migration) is ideal long-term but unrealistic for .NET Framework 4.x (requires significant refactoring). Option E suggests moving to Azure which doesn't answer the AWS migration question.

question:
A company's platform team supports 30 development teams deploying to AWS. They've standardized on EKS, Aurora, and DynamoDB. However, they're experiencing: inconsistent infrastructure provisioning (each team writes their own Terraform with varying quality), security misconfigurations discovered in production, slow onboarding of new services (2-3 weeks to set up infrastructure for a new microservice), and difficulty enforcing organizational standards across autonomous teams without becoming a bottleneck.

Which platform engineering approach BEST enables developer self-service while maintaining standards?

options.A: Create a shared Terraform module library. Require all teams to use approved modules. Platform team reviews all Terraform PRs. Implement Terraform Cloud with Sentinel policies for compliance checking.
options.B: AWS Service Catalog with pre-approved product portfolios. Products for common patterns (EKS namespace + Aurora database + DynamoDB table + CI/CD pipeline). Constraint policies for security standards. Self-service provisioning via Service Catalog console. TagOptions for standardized tagging.
options.C: Build an Internal Developer Platform (IDP) with Backstage as the frontend. Backstage templates provision infrastructure via Crossplane (Kubernetes-native IaC). Templates encode organizational standards (networking, security, observability). Developers self-serve through Backstage catalog, selecting a template that creates everything needed for a new service in minutes. OPA (Open Policy Agent) for policy enforcement on Crossplane resources. Platform team maintains templates, not individual deployments.
options.D: Implement AWS Proton for environment and service template management. Platform team defines environment templates (EKS clusters, networking). Service templates define the infrastructure each microservice needs. Developers deploy services via Proton, which provisions infrastructure from templates. AWS-managed template rendering and deployment.
options.E: Standardize on AWS CDK with custom constructs library published to CodeArtifact. Mandatory constructs for security (encryption, logging, access controls). CI/CD pipeline validates CDK output against Config rules before deployment. Teams compose constructs freely but mandatory ones are always included.

explanation: An Internal Developer Platform with Backstage + Crossplane provides: 1) Self-service — developers choose a template and get a fully configured service in minutes (solves the 2-3 week onboarding). 2) Standards enforcement — templates embed organizational best practices by construction (security, networking, observability are baked in). 3) Autonomy without bottleneck — platform team maintains templates, not individual deployments. Teams don't need PR approval for standard deployments. 4) Policy enforcement — OPA validates all resources against policies at deployment time. 5) Kubernetes-native (Crossplane) aligns with their EKS investment. Option A (Terraform PR reviews) makes the platform team a bottleneck. Option B (Service Catalog) works but provides less flexibility and developer experience than Backstage. Option D (Proton) is AWS-specific and has less ecosystem adoption than Backstage/Crossplane. Option E (CDK constructs) still requires each team to write infrastructure code.

question:
A financial institution needs to implement a system for regulatory reporting that aggregates transaction data from 15 different internal systems, applies complex transformation rules, generates reports in specific formats required by 8 different regulatory bodies (each with different schemas, frequencies, and submission methods), and maintains a complete audit trail of every generated report. Reports must be generated within 4 hours of the reporting deadline. If a regulator requests a historical report regeneration, the system must produce the exact same output as the original submission.

Which architecture ensures deterministic, auditable report generation?

options.A: AWS Glue ETL for data aggregation from source systems. Step Functions orchestrating report generation per regulator. Lambda functions applying transformation rules. S3 for storing generated reports with versioning. DynamoDB for tracking report generation metadata and submission status.
options.B: Event-driven architecture: EventBridge Scheduler triggers report generation. Step Functions orchestrates the pipeline. Source data snapshot to S3 (immutable copy with timestamp). Glue ETL applies deterministic transformations (no timestamps or random elements — version-pinned rule sets). Generated reports stored in S3 with Object Lock. Report metadata and transformation rule versions stored in DynamoDB. Submission via SFTP/API per regulator requirements automated through Step Functions. CloudTrail + report generation logs for complete audit trail.
options.C: Amazon MWAA (Airflow) DAGs for scheduling and orchestration per regulator. EMR for data processing. Custom Python scripts for report formatting. RDS for metadata storage. S3 for output. Manual submission to regulators.
options.D: Real-time streaming via Kinesis from source systems. Continuously aggregating data in Redshift. Materialized views per regulator format. Automated exports to S3 at deadline minus 4 hours. Email-based submission.
options.E: AWS Data Pipeline for ETL orchestration. Redshift for aggregation and transformation. UNLOAD to S3 for report files. Lambda for format conversion per regulator. SNS notifications on completion. Manual regulatory submission.

explanation: Deterministic report regeneration requires: 1) Immutable source data snapshots — S3 copies of source data at point-in-time ensure historical regeneration uses the same inputs. 2) Version-pinned transformation rules — storing the exact rule version used for each report ensures the same logic is applied. 3) No non-deterministic elements (timestamps, random IDs, float imprecision) in transformations. 4) Object Lock on generated reports ensures originals can't be tampered with (regulatory requirement). 5) Complete metadata trail linking report → rule version → source snapshot enables exact reproduction. Step Functions provides reliable orchestration with error handling. Automated submission per regulator requirements. Option A doesn't address deterministic regeneration (no source snapshotting or rule versioning). Option C uses manual submission and lacks immutability controls. Option D (real-time) makes point-in-time reproduction difficult. Option E (Data Pipeline) is legacy and lacks the audit controls.

question:
A company is implementing an AWS Control Tower landing zone for 150 accounts. They have specific requirements: certain workload accounts must only deploy resources that are encrypted with customer-managed KMS keys, some accounts process healthcare data requiring additional HIPAA controls, and all accounts must send logs to a centralized SIEM. The company also needs to allow exceptions for sandbox accounts where developers experiment freely. They want to manage this with minimum ongoing effort as new accounts are created.

Which governance model MOST effectively balances control with flexibility?

options.A: Control Tower with mandatory guardrails only. Custom Config rules deployed via StackSets for HIPAA accounts. Manual SCP application for encryption requirements. Sandbox accounts in a permissive OU with no guardrails.
options.B: Control Tower with Account Factory customizations. Register existing OUs. Use Control Tower lifecycle events to automatically apply configurations when accounts are created or moved between OUs.
options.C: Control Tower with OU-based governance: Production OU (encryption SCPs + all guardrails), Healthcare OU (encryption SCPs + HIPAA Config conformance pack + additional guardrails), Sandbox OU (minimal guardrails, no encryption requirement). Customizations for Control Tower (CfCT) deploying: centralized logging integration (Firehose to SIEM) to all accounts, KMS key creation in encryption-required OUs, HIPAA-specific Config rules to Healthcare OU. Account Factory provisions accounts into appropriate OU automatically applying all controls. New accounts inherit OU-level governance immediately.
options.D: Skip Control Tower. Use Organizations with SCPs manually. Deploy all configurations via Terraform. Manage compliance through CI/CD pipelines that validate account configurations before deployment.
