options.E: Control Tower with all available guardrails enabled globally. Use account-level exceptions via tag-based SCP conditions for sandbox accounts. HIPAA and encryption handled by service-level configurations rather than account-level controls.

explanation: OU-based governance with Control Tower provides automatic, inheritable controls: 1) Encryption requirement is enforced via SCP at the Production/Healthcare OU level — any new account placed there automatically inherits it. 2) HIPAA controls are deployed only to the Healthcare OU via CfCT (conformance pack + additional Config rules). 3) Sandbox OU has minimal restrictions for experimentation. 4) CfCT automatically deploys centralized logging to ALL accounts regardless of OU. 5) Account Factory ensures new accounts land in the correct OU with all controls applied immediately — zero manual effort for governance. Option A requires manual SCP application (doesn't meet 'minimum ongoing effort'). Option B mentions lifecycle events but doesn't specify the governance structure. Option D (no Control Tower) loses the automated governance inheritance. Option E (all guardrails globally) over-restricts sandbox accounts.

question:
A ride-sharing company needs to build a real-time matching system that pairs riders with drivers. The system must: find the closest available driver to a rider within 3 seconds, handle 50,000 concurrent ride requests, account for real-time traffic conditions when estimating arrival times, support ride-pooling (matching multiple riders going in similar directions), and maintain driver location updates every 2 seconds from 200,000 active drivers.

Which architecture provides real-time matching at this scale?

options.A: ElastiCache Redis with geospatial indexes (GEOADD/GEOSEARCH) for driver locations. Lambda for matching logic. DynamoDB for ride state. API Gateway WebSocket for rider/driver communication. Amazon Location Service for ETA calculation.
options.B: Custom matching engine on ECS with in-memory geospatial index (H3 hexagonal grid). Kinesis Data Streams for driver location updates. DynamoDB for ride and driver state. AWS AppSync for real-time subscriptions. Amazon Location Service Routes API for traffic-aware ETAs. Step Functions for ride-pooling matching orchestration.
options.C: Amazon OpenSearch with geo-point queries for driver search. Kinesis for location ingestion. Lambda for matching. DynamoDB for state. SNS for notifications to drivers. Google Maps API for traffic-aware routing.
options.D: DynamoDB with geohash-based GSI for proximity queries. DynamoDB Streams triggering Lambda for matching. SQS for ride request queue. API Gateway for client communication. HERE routing API for ETAs.
options.E: Amazon Neptune graph database modeling driver-rider-route relationships. Graph queries for optimal matching. Kinesis for location updates writing to Neptune. Lambda for business logic. Custom routing engine on EC2.

explanation: At 200,000 drivers updating every 2 seconds (100K updates/sec) with 50K concurrent matching requests needing sub-3-second response, the system needs: 1) Custom matching engine on ECS with in-memory geospatial index — H3 hexagonal grid enables O(1) lookups of nearby drivers and efficient ride-pooling (comparing route directions within a hex). In-memory is essential for the latency requirement. 2) Kinesis handles the 100K/sec location update throughput. 3) Amazon Location Service Routes API provides traffic-aware ETAs without building a custom routing engine. 4) AppSync subscriptions push real-time updates to riders/drivers. 5) Step Functions orchestrates the more complex ride-pooling matching (multi-step optimization). Option A (Redis geospatial) works for simple nearest-driver but doesn't handle ride-pooling direction matching well. Option C (OpenSearch) adds query latency inappropriate for 3-second SLA at this scale. Option D (DynamoDB geohash) requires scanning multiple hash prefixes for proximity. Option E (Neptune) isn't designed for real-time location data at this update rate.

question: A company's data transfer costs are $200,000/month and growing 20% quarterly. Breakdown: $80K inter-region transfer (disaster recovery replication, multi-region deployments), $60K internet egress (API responses to customers, software downloads), $35K inter-AZ transfer (microservices communication across AZs), and $25K NAT Gateway data processing (container pulls, API calls to AWS services, third-party API calls). Which combination of optimizations provides the GREATEST data transfer cost reduction?

options.A: CloudFront for customer-facing APIs and software downloads (reduced egress pricing). VPC endpoints for AWS service API calls (eliminates NAT Gateway processing for those calls). Deploy single-AZ for non-critical services. Use S3 Transfer Acceleration for inter-region data movement.
options.B: CloudFront for all internet egress (API responses + downloads). Gateway VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB (free). Interface VPC endpoints for other AWS services (eliminates NAT processing). Deploy services in single AZ where HA isn't required. Implement gRPC with compression for inter-service communication (reduces bytes transferred). Evaluate whether inter-region replication can use S3 same-region replication class or reduced-frequency sync.
options.C: AWS Global Accelerator for customer APIs. Direct Connect for all traffic. PrivateLink for service-to-service communication. Compress all payloads.
options.D: Negotiate a custom pricing agreement with AWS for data transfer. Implement aggressive caching at every layer. Move to single-region architecture to eliminate inter-region transfer.
options.E: Replace NAT Gateways with NAT instances. Use Transit Gateway for inter-region peering (same price per GB). Implement API response compression. Deploy CloudFront for downloads only.

explanation: This targets all four cost categories: 1) CloudFront reduces internet egress from $0.09/GB to $0.085/GB (lower tiers at scale) and caches repeat requests (could cut $60K by 40-60%). 2) Gateway VPC endpoints for S3/DynamoDB are FREE (eliminate NAT processing charges for the highest-volume AWS API calls). Interface VPC endpoints for other services (~$0.01/GB vs $0.045/GB NAT processing — 78% cheaper). 3) Single-AZ for non-critical services eliminates $0.01/GB inter-AZ charges where HA isn't needed. 4) gRPC + compression reduces payload sizes by 60-80% for inter-service calls (reducing both inter-AZ and inter-region bytes). 5) Evaluating DR replication frequency may reduce inter-region costs. Option A is similar but doesn't address compression or inter-region optimization. Option C (Global Accelerator) doesn't reduce data transfer pricing. Option D suggests single-region which impacts DR. Option E's NAT instances are a reliability risk.

question:
A company has 50 production AWS accounts with varying levels of Infrastructure as Code adoption. Some teams use Terraform, some use CloudFormation, and some still create resources manually via the console. This has resulted in: configuration drift (manual changes diverging from code), inability to recreate environments consistently, no clear ownership of resources, and difficulty in disaster recovery (can't reliably rebuild from code). The company wants to achieve 100% IaC coverage without disrupting teams that prefer different tools.

Which strategy MOST effectively achieves universal IaC adoption while respecting existing tool choices?

options.A: Mandate Terraform for all teams. Provide a 6-month transition period. Import existing resources using terraform import. Implement Terraform Cloud with state management and policy as code. Reject any changes not made through Terraform.
options.B: Allow teams to keep their preferred IaC tool (Terraform, CloudFormation, CDK). Implement AWS Config rules detecting resources not managed by IaC (missing aws:cloudformation:stack-id tag or terraform state tracking). Use AWS CloudFormation drift detection for CF-managed resources and terraform plan for TF-managed resources. For manually-created resources: use former2 (or cf-to-tf) to generate IaC from existing resources, then import. Implement SCP that requires a 'ManagedBy' tag on all new resources. EventBridge rule triggering alerts for any resource creation not from an IaC pipeline (detect by checking if the event source is CloudFormation, Terraform runner role, etc.).
options.C: Deploy AWS Service Catalog as the only way to create resources. All teams must use Service Catalog products. Import existing resources into CloudFormation stacks backing Service Catalog products.
options.D: Use AWS Cloud Control API as a universal IaC layer. All tools interact through Cloud Control. Implement Config rules for compliance. Let teams use any tool that supports Cloud Control.
options.E: Implement GitOps for everything. All infrastructure defined in Git. ArgoCD/Flux for Kubernetes resources. Atlantis for Terraform. CloudFormation Git sync for CF. Any resource not in a Git repository is flagged for import or deletion.

explanation: This approach respects tool diversity while achieving the goal: 1) Allowing existing tools avoids resistance and retraining (teams are productive with their chosen tool). 2) Detection of unmanaged resources (via Config rules checking for IaC tags) identifies gaps. 3) Generation tools (former2, cf-to-tf) create IaC definitions from existing manual resources — enabling import without recreation. 4) SCP-enforced 'ManagedBy' tag requirement ensures all new resources are tracked. 5) EventBridge detection of non-IaC resource creation provides real-time alerts when someone bypasses IaC. 6) Drift detection ensures IaC stays in sync with actual state. Option A (mandate Terraform) causes disruption and ignores existing CloudFormation investments. Option C (Service Catalog only) is too restrictive. Option D (Cloud Control) is a protocol, not a governance solution. Option E (GitOps) is an operational model that still needs detection/enforcement mechanisms.

question:
A company is building a platform for processing satellite imagery. They receive 10 TB of imagery data daily from multiple satellite operators. The pipeline must: ingest raw imagery, apply radiometric and geometric corrections, run object detection ML models (identifying buildings, roads, vegetation), generate change detection by comparing with historical imagery, produce tiled map layers (vector tiles and raster tiles), and serve the processed imagery to 1,000 GIS analysts via a web application with sub-second pan/zoom performance.

Which architecture handles the processing and serving requirements?

options.A: S3 for raw and processed imagery. AWS Batch with GPU instances for ML inference (object detection). Lambda for lightweight transformations. SageMaker for model training and batch transform. CloudFront + S3 for serving tiles. OpenSearch for spatial metadata queries. Amazon Location Service for base maps.
options.B: S3 for data lake. EMR with GeoSpark for spatial processing. SageMaker endpoints for object detection. Custom tile server on ECS. CloudFront for tile caching. PostGIS on RDS for spatial queries. OpenSearch for text/metadata search.
options.C: S3 for imagery storage (raw + processed + tiles). AWS Batch with GPU instances (p3/g4dn) for ML inference and radiometric/geometric corrections. Step Functions orchestrating the pipeline (ingest → correct → detect → change detection → tile generation). SageMaker for model training. Lambda + GDAL layer for tile generation. CloudFront + S3 for raster tile serving. Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with PostGIS extension for vector data and spatial queries. Mapbox Vector Tiles generated and cached on CloudFront for web application.
options.D: Kinesis Data Streams for real-time imagery ingestion. Kinesis Data Analytics for stream processing. SageMaker real-time endpoints for per-image ML inference. DynamoDB with geohash for spatial indexing. S3 for archival. EC2 with GeoServer for map serving.
options.E: S3 for storage. AWS Glue for ETL processing of imagery metadata. Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels for object detection. Athena for spatial queries using GeoJSON. Amplify for the web application. CloudFront for content delivery.

explanation: This architecture handles the full satellite imagery pipeline: S3 provides cost-effective storage for the multi-TB daily ingest. AWS Batch with GPU instances handles both the compute-intensive corrections (radiometric/geometric) and ML inference at batch scale (more cost-effective than real-time endpoints for 10 TB daily). Step Functions orchestrates the multi-stage pipeline with error handling and retry. Lambda + GDAL generates standard map tiles efficiently. CloudFront + S3 serves tiles globally with sub-second performance (pre-generated tiles served from edge). PostGIS on Aurora handles the complex spatial queries (intersections, buffers, nearest-neighbor) that GIS analysts need. Vector tiles on CloudFront enable smooth pan/zoom. Option A doesn't address the spatial query needs adequately. Option B (EMR GeoSpark) adds cluster management overhead. Option D (streaming) is inappropriate for batch satellite imagery. Option E (Rekognition) doesn't support custom satellite imagery models.

question:
A company is implementing a data mesh architecture on AWS where each of 15 business domains owns their data products. Each domain team needs to publish data products that other domains can discover and consume. They need a federated governance model where a central team defines global policies (data classification, retention, PII handling) but domain teams manage their own data quality and access policies. Data consumers should be able to find and request access to data products through a self-service catalog.

Which architecture BEST implements a data mesh on AWS?

options.A: Each domain has its own AWS account with S3 data lake. AWS Lake Formation for cross-account data sharing. Central governance account with Lake Formation managing global policies. Glue Data Catalog in each domain for local metadata. AWS DataZone for the data product catalog and access request workflow.
options.B: Single centralized data lake in S3 with prefix-based domain separation. Lake Formation tag-based access control for domain isolation. Glue Data Catalog with database-per-domain structure. Custom portal built on Amplify for data discovery. SNS-based approval workflow for access requests.
options.C: Amazon DataZone as the federated catalog spanning all domain accounts. Each domain publishes data products to DataZone with quality metrics and SLAs. DataZone manages subscriptions (access requests and approvals). Lake Formation in each domain account for fine-grained access control with central governance policies inherited from management account. Glue Data Catalog as the technical metadata store per domain. Central Lake Formation administrator defines global LF-Tags (classification, PII-level) that domain administrators use when granting access. Cross-account data sharing via Lake Formation + RAM.
options.D: Apache Atlas on EMR for metadata management. Kafka on MSK for data product event bus. Custom data product registry on DynamoDB. Airflow (MWAA) for data pipeline orchestration per domain. Cross-account roles for data access.
options.E: AWS Glue Data Catalog as centralized metadata store with IAM-based access per domain. S3 Access Points per consumer for data access. CloudFormation for provisioning data sharing infrastructure. No self-service catalog (teams email the data platform team for access).

explanation: Data mesh requires: domain ownership, federated governance, data as a product, and self-serve infrastructure. This architecture maps perfectly: 1) DataZone provides the self-service catalog where domains publish and consumers discover/subscribe — the core data mesh marketplace. 2) Per-domain accounts with their own Glue Catalog and Lake Formation enable true domain ownership. 3) Central Lake Formation administrator defines global LF-Tags (classification, PII) that all domains must use — federated governance without central bottleneck. 4) DataZone subscription management handles access request workflows without central team involvement. 5) Cross-account sharing via Lake Formation + RAM provides secure consumption. Option A is similar but doesn't detail the federated governance model with LF-Tags. Option B (single centralized lake) contradicts data mesh principles. Option D uses non-managed tools adding operational burden. Option E has no self-service.

question:
A company is building a compliance monitoring system that must continuously verify that all AWS resources across 100 accounts comply with 500 custom rules. The rules range from simple checks (S3 bucket encryption enabled) to complex multi-resource validations (verify that every public-facing ALB has a WAF ACL attached AND the WAF ACL contains specific rule groups AND those rule groups are configured with specific actions). Results must be queryable by auditors with filters on rule, account, region, resource type, and compliance status. Non-compliant resources must be flagged within 15 minutes of creation.

Which architecture provides comprehensive, timely compliance monitoring?

options.A: AWS Config with custom rules (Lambda-based) for all 500 rules. Config Aggregator for multi-account view. Config Advanced Query for auditor queries. EventBridge for near-real-time detection of resource changes. SNS for non-compliance notifications.
options.B: AWS Config organization-wide rules for simple checks. Custom Lambda functions invoked by EventBridge rules on CloudTrail events for complex multi-resource validations. Results stored in DynamoDB with GSIs for querying. OpenSearch Dashboards for auditor interface. Step Functions for multi-step validations.
options.C: AWS Config with organization-wide deployment for standard rules. Custom Config rules (Lambda) for complex multi-resource checks. Config Aggregator in a delegated administrator account. Amazon Athena querying Config snapshots in S3 for auditor ad-hoc queries. EventBridge integration with Config for near-real-time compliance status changes. Custom dashboard in QuickSight connected to Athena for auditor filtering and visualization. AWS Security Hub custom standards for aggregated compliance scoring.
options.D: Deploy Prowler (open-source) on a scheduled Lambda or Fargate task scanning all accounts hourly. Store results in S3. Athena for querying. Custom dashboard for auditors. EventBridge Scheduler for the hourly scans.
options.E: AWS Audit Manager with custom control sets mapping to the 500 rules. Automated evidence collection from Config, CloudTrail, and Security Hub. Audit Manager assessment reports for auditors. Manual remediation workflows.

explanation: This architecture addresses all requirements: 1) Config organization-wide rules provide automatic deployment of rules across 100 accounts without per-account management. 2) Custom Config rules (Lambda) handle complex multi-resource validations by querying related resources during evaluation. 3) Config's continuous recording + EventBridge integration detects non-compliance within minutes of resource changes (meeting 15-minute SLA). 4) Config Aggregator centralizes compliance data. 5) Athena on Config snapshots enables flexible ad-hoc querying by auditors with any filter combination. 6) QuickSight provides the visual dashboard with drill-down. 7) Security Hub custom standards provide an executive-level compliance score. Option A is similar but Config Advanced Query has limitations compared to Athena on snapshots for complex auditor queries. Option B requires custom event-driven infrastructure instead of leveraging Config's built-in continuous evaluation. Option D (hourly Prowler) doesn't meet 15-minute SLA. Option E (Audit Manager) is designed for periodic assessments, not continuous real-time monitoring.

question:
A company operates a large Hadoop ecosystem on-premises (200 node cluster) running: HDFS (3 PB data), Hive metastore (50,000 tables), Spark jobs (500 daily), HBase (200 TB for real-time lookups), Kafka (1 TB/hour throughput for streaming), and Airflow for orchestration. They want to migrate to AWS managed services to reduce operational overhead. The migration must be completed in 12 months with minimal disruption to the 200 data engineers and analysts who use the platform daily.

Which migration strategy provides the smoothest transition to AWS managed services?

options.A: Lift and shift entire Hadoop cluster to EMR. Same tools, same interfaces. Once stable on EMR, incrementally replace components: HDFS→S3, Hive Metastore→Glue Catalog, HBase→DynamoDB, Kafka→MSK, Airflow→MWAA.
options.B: Big bang migration: Replace everything simultaneously — S3 for storage, Glue for ETL, Athena for queries, DynamoDB for real-time, MSK for streaming, MWAA for orchestration. Retrain all 200 users.
options.C:
Phase 1 (months 1-3): Set up AWS infrastructure. Migrate HDFS to S3 using DistCp/DataSync. Set up AWS Glue Data Catalog as a federated Hive Metastore (compatible with existing Hive/Spark queries). Deploy MSK and mirror Kafka topics using MirrorMaker 2.

Phase 2 (months 4-8): Migrate Spark jobs to EMR (minimal changes — Spark on S3 with Glue Catalog works like Spark on HDFS with Hive Metastore). Migrate HBase to Amazon EMR HBase with S3 storage (same API, users notice nothing). Cut over Kafka consumers to MSK.

Phase 3 (months 9-12): Migrate Airflow DAGs to MWAA (compatible — same Airflow operators). Incrementally migrate Spark jobs from EMR to Glue for fully serverless operation where appropriate. Migrate HBase to DynamoDB for workloads that benefit from it (with application changes).

Throughout: Dual-run with data validation.
options.D: Migrate only storage (HDFS→S3) and keep everything else on-premises connecting to S3 via Direct Connect. Phase 2 (year 2) migrates compute.
options.E: Use AWS Outposts to run the Hadoop cluster on AWS infrastructure in the company's data center. This provides AWS management without migration complexity. Plan a future full migration.

explanation: This phased approach minimizes disruption because each phase preserves familiar interfaces: Phase 1 moves data transparently (S3 accessed like HDFS via s3a://, Glue Catalog is Hive Metastore-compatible). Phase 2 migrates compute with minimal code changes (Spark queries work the same with Glue Catalog as they did with Hive Metastore). Phase 3 modernizes orchestration (MWAA runs the same DAGs) and optionally moves to fully serverless. Throughout, dual-running validates correctness. Users barely change their workflows at each phase. Option A (lift-and-shift then modernize) takes longer and runs a full EMR cluster initially (more expensive). Option B (big bang) is extremely high risk with 200 users. Option D only partially migrates. Option E doesn't actually migrate to AWS services.

question:
A company needs to build a system that detects and responds to security threats across their AWS infrastructure in near-real-time. The system must correlate events from multiple sources (CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, GuardDuty, Security Hub), detect complex attack patterns that span multiple events over time windows (e.g., reconnaissance followed by privilege escalation followed by data exfiltration), and automatically execute response playbooks when high-confidence threats are detected.

Which architecture provides the MOST effective automated threat detection and response?

options.A: Amazon Security Lake for centralized log aggregation (OCSF format). Amazon OpenSearch for log analysis and correlation. Custom detection rules in OpenSearch using alerting plugin. Lambda for automated response actions. Step Functions for playbook orchestration.
options.B: GuardDuty for threat detection. Security Hub for aggregation. EventBridge rules triggering Lambda for automated responses. CloudWatch Logs Insights for ad-hoc investigation. Custom GuardDuty threat lists for organization-specific indicators.
options.C: Amazon Security Lake aggregating all log sources in OCSF format. Amazon Detective for investigation and visualization of entity relationships. GuardDuty for ML-based threat detection. Security Hub for centralized findings. EventBridge rules routing high-severity findings to Step Functions playbooks that execute automated responses (isolate instance, revoke credentials, snapshot for forensics). Custom threat detection using Athena scheduled queries on Security Lake data for multi-event pattern correlation with time windows. SNS for SOC team notification on medium-confidence detections.
options.D: Splunk on EC2 for SIEM functionality. Splunk SOAR for automated response playbooks. Ingest all AWS logs via Kinesis Data Firehose. Custom correlation rules and ML models in Splunk.
options.E: CloudTrail Lake for event analysis. Amazon Macie for data exposure detection. GuardDuty for threats. Individual EventBridge rules per threat type triggering specific Lambda response functions. No centralized correlation — each service detects its own threat category independently.

explanation: This architecture provides defense in depth with automated response: 1) Security Lake centralizes all log sources in the standard OCSF format enabling cross-source correlation. 2) GuardDuty provides ML-based detection for known threat patterns (continuously improving). 3) Athena scheduled queries on Security Lake data enable custom multi-event correlation — detecting patterns like 'same principal did ListBuckets in 5 regions followed by AssumeRole to a privileged role followed by GetObject on sensitive bucket within 30 minutes.' 4) Step Functions playbooks provide reliable, auditable automated response with conditional logic (isolate vs alert depending on confidence). 5) Detective provides investigation visualization when analysts need to dig deeper. 6) Security Hub centralizes all findings. Option A lacks GuardDuty's ML detection. Option B doesn't provide multi-event correlation or centralized logging. Option D adds third-party operational overhead. Option E has no cross-source correlation capability.

question:
A company has implemented a comprehensive tagging strategy but struggles with tag compliance. Analysis shows 40% of their $3M monthly spend is on untagged or improperly tagged resources, making cost allocation to business units impossible. Resources are created by 500 developers across 50 accounts through various methods (console, CLI, IaC, SDK). The finance team cannot produce accurate chargeback reports and business unit leaders dispute their allocated costs.

Which approach MOST effectively achieves and maintains tag compliance across all resource creation methods?

options.A: AWS Organizations tag policies for tag value standardization. SCPs denying resource creation without required tags. AWS Config rules for detection. Monthly reports to team leads showing their compliance percentage.
options.B: Implement tag policies via Organizations for standardization. Deploy SCPs requiring tags (CostCenter, Owner, Environment) on all taggable resource creation APIs — with a condition key check for required tags. Supplement with a 'tag-on-create' Lambda triggered by CloudTrail events that auto-tags resources with creator information when tags are missing (defense in depth for resources where SCPs can't enforce). AWS Config organization-wide rule detecting untagged resources with auto-remediation via SSM Automation. Monthly chargeback reports allocated by CostCenter tag. Untagged costs allocated to the creating account's default cost center with a surcharge (incentive).
options.C: Implement AWS Service Catalog as the only resource creation method. All Service Catalog products require tag inputs. Deny all other resource creation methods via SCPs.
options.D: Use AWS Cost Categories to allocate costs based on account + tag combinations. For untagged resources, use inherited tags from parent resources. Implement tagging via IaC templates only (require PR review for tag compliance). Accept some untagged resources and handle in monthly reconciliation.
options.E: Deploy Tag Editor to bulk-tag existing resources. Train developers on tagging requirements. Implement weekly automated reports showing untagged resources. Name and shame non-compliant teams in all-hands meetings.

explanation: This provides multiple enforcement layers covering all creation methods: 1) Tag policies standardize tag key names and allowed values (preventing typos like 'cost-center' vs 'CostCenter'). 2) SCPs enforce tag presence at creation time — resources literally cannot be created without required tags via any method (console, CLI, SDK, IaC). This is the strongest control. 3) CloudTrail-triggered Lambda catches edge cases where SCPs can't enforce (some resource types don't support tag-on-create). 4) Config + SSM Automation catches anything that slips through and auto-remediates. 5) Financial incentive (surcharge on untagged costs) motivates teams to maintain compliance proactively. Option A is similar but lacks the auto-tagging backup and financial incentive. Option C (Service Catalog only) is too restrictive for 500 developers. Option D accepts non-compliance. Option E relies on social pressure rather than technical enforcement.

question:
A company needs to implement a data pipeline that processes sensitive PII data (social security numbers, credit card numbers, health records) from 100 source systems. The pipeline must automatically detect and classify PII in unstructured data, apply appropriate de-identification (tokenization, masking, or encryption depending on the data type and consumer), maintain the ability to re-identify data when legally required, and provide different views of the same data to different consumers based on their authorization level (full PII for compliance team, tokenized for analytics, fully masked for ML training).

Which architecture provides comprehensive PII management?

options.A: Amazon Macie for PII detection in S3 data. Lambda for de-identification rules. Multiple S3 buckets per authorization level (full, tokenized, masked). IAM policies controlling access to appropriate bucket.
options.B: AWS Glue with PII detection transforms for classification during ETL. Glue applies de-identification (tokenization using custom KMS-backed token mapping in DynamoDB, format-preserving encryption for SSNs, masking for ML datasets). Lake Formation tag-based access control with column-level security — same table, different columns visible based on consumer's LF-Tag grant. Token vault in DynamoDB with KMS encryption for re-identification (accessible only to compliance team via IAM + Lake Formation permissions). Multiple Glue job outputs writing de-identified variants to the same Iceberg table with different column classifications.
options.C: Amazon Comprehend for PII entity detection. Custom Lambda pipeline for de-identification. Separate databases per consumer type. Vault service on ECS for tokenization and re-identification. Complex access control via application-level middleware.
options.D: Amazon Macie for detection. AWS Clean Rooms for analytics consumers (computes on data without exposing raw PII). Separate masked copies for ML training. Manual process for compliance team access to full PII.
options.E: Third-party DLP tool (BigID/Informatica) on EC2 for PII detection and classification. Custom tokenization microservice. Multiple data stores per consumer. API gateway controlling access based on consumer identity.

explanation: This architecture provides unified PII management: 1) Glue PII detection transforms automatically identify PII during ETL (built-in entity recognition for SSN, credit card, etc. in structured/unstructured data). 2) Multiple de-identification methods applied contextually — tokenization preserves referential integrity for analytics, format-preserving encryption maintains field format, masking removes PII entirely for ML. 3) Lake Formation tag-based access control provides column-level security on a single table — the compliance team sees all columns, analytics sees tokenized columns, ML team sees masked columns. No data duplication needed. 4) Token vault in DynamoDB with strict access controls enables re-identification when legally required. 5) Single Iceberg table with multiple column-level access grants is operationally simpler than maintaining multiple copies. Option A requires multiple copies (storage cost, synchronization). Option C adds application-level complexity. Option D (Clean Rooms) is for cross-organization analytics, not internal access control. Option E adds third-party dependency.

question:
A company is implementing a disaster recovery strategy for their entire AWS landing zone (100 accounts, 3 regions). They need to recover not just application workloads but the entire control plane: Organizations structure, SCPs, IAM Identity Center configurations, networking (Transit Gateway, Direct Connect), and shared services. The RTO for the control plane is 4 hours. They've experienced an incident where a misconfigured SCP locked out all accounts from a critical region.

Which approach ensures control plane recoverability?

options.A: Infrastructure as Code for everything. Store all Organization, SCP, IAM Identity Center, and networking configurations in Git. Use CI/CD to deploy changes. In a disaster, redeploy from Git. Regular terraform plan to detect drift.
options.B: AWS Backup for all supported resources. Cross-region backup copies. Regular DR drills. Manual runbooks for control plane recovery.
options.C: IaC (Terraform/CloudFormation) for all control plane configurations stored in a separate recovery account and S3 (versioned, cross-region replicated). Automated pipeline that continuously validates control plane state matches IaC. Break-glass mechanism: a recovery account (separate from the Organization) with cross-account roles that can modify SCPs/Organization settings even if Identity Center is down. Regular automated DR drills that actually test recovery. CloudTrail organization trail with real-time alerting on SCP/Organization changes (early detection of misconfiguration).
options.D: AWS Control Tower handles Organization management. Trust that AWS maintains the control plane. Focus DR efforts on application workloads only. Implement manual break-glass procedures documented in a wiki.
options.E: Multi-Organization strategy: maintain a shadow Organization with identical structure, SCPs, and networking. Synchronize changes between primary and shadow Organizations via Lambda. In disaster, switch all workloads to shadow Organization.

explanation: Control plane DR requires special considerations beyond application DR: 1) IaC for all control plane configurations enables rebuild from code. 2) Separate recovery account outside the Organization is critical — if an SCP locks out access or Identity Center fails, you need an independent access path. Cross-account roles in this recovery account bypass Organization-level controls. 3) Continuous state validation detects drift (like the SCP misconfiguration) before it becomes a disaster. 4) Automated DR drills prove recovery works within 4-hour RTO. 5) Real-time SCP change alerting enables rapid response to misconfiguration. Option A (IaC only) doesn't address the break-glass access problem — if SCPs lock you out, you can't run your IaC pipeline. Option B (AWS Backup) doesn't cover control plane resources. Option D trusts AWS for control plane which doesn't protect against self-inflicted misconfigurations. Option E (shadow Organization) is extremely complex and expensive to maintain.

question:
A company is building a platform for running clinical trials that must comply with 21 CFR Part 11 (electronic records and signatures). The platform must: capture patient data from multiple sites, ensure data cannot be modified after submission without a full audit trail, support electronic signatures with legal validity, maintain system access controls with periodic re-certification, produce tamper-evident audit trails for FDA inspection, and retain all data for 15+ years with guaranteed integrity.

Which architecture meets 21 CFR Part 11 requirements?

options.A: API Gateway + Lambda for data capture. DynamoDB with point-in-time recovery for data storage. CloudTrail for audit. Cognito for authentication with MFA. S3 with versioning for document storage.
options.B: ECS Fargate for application tier with mutual TLS. Aurora PostgreSQL with audit trigger logging every row change (old value, new value, who, when). S3 Object Lock (Compliance mode, 15-year retention) for submitted documents and audit logs. IAM Identity Center with MFA for system access. Custom electronic signature workflow using KMS asymmetric keys (signer's private key signs the record hash — verifiable non-repudiation). Quarterly access review automation via Lambda checking last access dates. Amazon QLDB for the immutable audit trail of all data modifications.
options.C: Blockchain (Amazon Managed Blockchain) for all patient data storage (inherently immutable). Smart contracts for electronic signatures. IPFS for document storage. Ethereum-based access control.
options.D: Standard web application on EC2. MySQL database with application-level audit logging. Digital signatures using third-party DocuSign API. S3 for document retention. IAM users for access control.
options.E: Serverless architecture with Lambda. DynamoDB with streams writing to a Kinesis-based audit log. Cognito with custom MFA. S3 Glacier Deep Archive for 15-year retention. Custom signing solution using AWS Signer.

explanation: 21 CFR Part 11 has specific requirements that this architecture addresses: 1) Audit trail — QLDB provides a cryptographically verifiable, immutable journal of all changes (purpose-built for this). Aurora audit triggers capture field-level changes with who/when. 2) Electronic signatures — KMS asymmetric keys provide non-repudiation (the signer's key signs the record hash, provably linking signer to content). The signature is mathematically bound to the data. 3) Data integrity — S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode guarantees no modification or deletion for 15 years (even by root). 4) Access controls — Identity Center with MFA, plus automated quarterly access reviews satisfy the re-certification requirement. 5) Tamper-evident audit — QLDB's hash-chained journal is verifiable by FDA inspectors. Option A lacks immutable audit trail and proper e-signatures. Option C (blockchain) is over-engineered and doesn't map well to clinical trial workflows. Option D (application-level audit) is modifiable by administrators. Option E lacks immutable audit and proper signature mechanisms.

question:
A company has a development environment that is an exact replica of production. Production runs 200 services on EKS spending $400K/month. The development environment also spends $400K/month because it mirrors production exactly. Developers argue they need a full production replica for accurate testing. However, analysis shows: development environment CPU utilization averages 8%, most services in dev are idle (only the service being tested and its immediate dependencies are active at any time), and only 30 developers actively use dev at any time (but all claim they need instant access).

What is the MOST cost-effective approach that still provides developers with production-representative testing?

options.A: Reduce all pod resource requests in dev by 75%. Run on Spot instances. Implement cluster autoscaler with aggressive scale-down. This maintains all 200 services running but at minimal resource.
options.B: Implement ephemeral environments: each developer gets a full environment only when they need it, automatically torn down after testing. Use namespace-per-developer on a shared EKS cluster with minimal always-on shared services.
options.C: Implement a service virtualization approach: keep a minimal always-on 'skeleton' (API Gateway + service mesh routing + databases with production-like data). When a developer needs to test, only their service and its direct dependencies run as real containers (scaled up from zero via KEDA). All other upstream/downstream services are replaced with lightweight service mocks/stubs that return production-representative responses. Shared dev cluster on Spot instances with Karpenter. Automatically scale to zero after 30 minutes of inactivity per service.
options.D: Maintain the full dev environment but shut it down outside business hours (save 65%). During business hours, run at 50% pod count. Use Savings Plans for the reduced steady-state cost.
options.E: Eliminate the shared dev environment. Each developer runs services locally using Docker Compose with production container images. Provide a small staging environment for integration testing before production.

explanation: Service virtualization provides production-representative testing at a fraction of the cost: 1) The always-on skeleton (API gateway, mesh, databases) costs very little but provides the routing and data layer. 2) KEDA scale-from-zero means only actively-tested services (and their immediate dependencies) consume compute — at 8% average utilization and 30 active developers, this might be 10-20 services at any time instead of 200. 3) Service mocks/stubs for uninvolved services provide realistic responses without running full containers (a mock returning representative JSON costs essentially nothing). 4) Spot + Karpenter + 30-minute auto-shutdown minimize even the running service costs. Expected savings: 85-90% ($340-360K/month). Option A still runs all 200 services (wasteful). Option B (full ephemeral environments) is expensive if each dev gets 200 services. Option D only saves 65% and still wastes during business hours. Option E (local Docker) doesn't provide production-representative networking/scale behavior.

question:
A company with 200 microservices on EKS is experiencing growing reliability concerns. Their current SLOs are frequently missed: 99.9% availability target is met only 97% of months, p99 latency target of 500ms is exceeded 20% of the time, and error rate target of 0.1% is breached weekly by at least one service. The team has no formal SLO framework — targets were set arbitrarily without measurement infrastructure. They also have no error budget policy, so there's no mechanism to slow down deployments when reliability is poor.

Which approach MOST effectively establishes and maintains reliability for the platform?

options.A: Implement Prometheus with custom SLI recording rules. Define SLOs in Sloth (SLO generator for Prometheus). Alert on error budget burn rate rather than raw metrics. Implement error budget policies: when budget is <25%, freeze deployments except bug fixes. Use Grafana dashboards showing SLO status and remaining error budget per service. Nobl9 or similar for SLO tracking over time. Publish SLO reports monthly to leadership.
options.B: Set stricter SLO targets (99.99% availability). Implement more monitoring and alerting. Require all services to pass load tests before deployment. Add redundancy to all services (3x current capacity). Hire more SREs.
options.C: Implement Amazon CloudWatch SLO feature (SLI metrics → SLO definitions). Set realistic SLOs based on 3 months of historical data (don't set aspirational targets — set achievable ones first). Define SLIs precisely: availability = successful requests / total requests (excluding health checks), latency = p99 of non-batch endpoints, error rate = 5xx / total. Implement tiered error budget policies: >50% budget remaining = deploy freely, 25-50% = require canary deployment, <25% = freeze non-critical deployments, 0% = incident mode. Alert on burn rate (fast burn = page, slow burn = ticket). Monthly SLO review meetings adjusting targets as reliability improves.
options.D: Deploy Istio service mesh for automatic retries and circuit breaking (improves measured reliability without fixing root causes). Implement adaptive request concurrency limiting. Add Envoy-level retry budgets. This mechanically improves SLI measurements.
options.E: Focus on the top 10 most critical services only. Set SLOs for those. Ignore the remaining 190 services. The 10 critical services get dedicated SRE attention, auto-scaling, and priority incident response.

explanation: This establishes a complete SLO practice from the ground up: 1) Setting SLOs based on historical data (not aspirational) means they're achievable and meaningful — you improve over time rather than permanently failing unrealistic targets. 2) Precise SLI definitions eliminate measurement ambiguity (excluding health checks prevents false positives). 3) Tiered error budget policies create a graduated response mechanism — the worse reliability gets, the more protective actions kick in. This naturally balances velocity with reliability. 4) Burn rate alerting detects SLO threats early (fast burn = 1-hour page, slow burn = multi-day ticket) rather than alerting after the SLO is already breached. 5) Monthly reviews create a feedback loop for tightening SLOs as teams improve. Option A is similar but Prometheus/Sloth adds operational overhead vs CloudWatch SLO (AWS-native). Option B sets unrealistic targets and throws resources at the problem. Option D masks problems with retries. Option E ignores 95% of services.

question:
A government weather agency needs to build a system that ingests weather data from 50,000 IoT sensors deployed across the country (temperature, humidity, pressure, wind, precipitation readings every 60 seconds), runs numerical weather prediction models (compute-intensive — requires 10,000 CPU cores for 30 minutes per forecast cycle, running every 6 hours), generates forecasts viewable on a public website serving 10 million daily visitors, and sends severe weather alerts to 100 million subscribers within 60 seconds of detection.

Which architecture handles all four requirements?

options.A: IoT Core for sensor ingestion. S3 for observation storage. AWS Batch with HPC instances for weather models. CloudFront + S3 for static forecast maps. SNS for alerts with high throughput mode.
options.B: IoT Core for sensor ingestion (50K devices × 1 msg/min = 833 msg/sec). Kinesis Data Streams for real-time observation pipeline. Amazon Timestream for observation time-series storage and real-time anomaly queries. AWS ParallelCluster with hpc7a instances (EFA-enabled) for numerical weather prediction. S3 for model output. CloudFront + Lambda@Edge for dynamic forecast rendering. Amazon Pinpoint for mass alert delivery (100M push/SMS within 60 seconds using campaign segmentation). Kinesis-triggered Lambda for severe weather detection from sensor anomalies.
options.C: Amazon MSK for sensor data ingestion. EMR for weather model computation. Redshift for forecast data storage. ECS for web application. Amazon SES for alerts via email only.
options.D: IoT Core for ingestion. DynamoDB for sensor data. EC2 Auto Scaling group for weather models. ELB + EC2 for public website. SQS + Lambda for alert fan-out.
options.E: Kinesis Data Streams for ingestion (bypassing IoT Core for throughput). Lambda for processing. SageMaker for ML-based weather prediction (replacing numerical models). API Gateway for forecast API. SNS for alerts.

explanation: This architecture addresses all four requirements at scale: 1) IoT Core handles 50K device connections with automatic scaling. Kinesis provides the streaming pipeline for real-time processing. 2) Timestream stores time-series observations efficiently with SQL queries for anomaly detection (severe weather triggers). 3) ParallelCluster with HPC instances and EFA provides the tightly-coupled compute needed for numerical weather prediction models (these require inter-node communication during computation). 4) CloudFront serves forecasts to 10M visitors with Lambda@Edge for dynamic content. 5) Amazon Pinpoint can deliver to 100M subscribers within the 60-second window using parallel delivery channels (push notifications, SMS). 6) Kinesis-triggered Lambda enables real-time severe weather detection from sensor anomalies. Option A (SNS) has throughput limits for 100M subscribers in 60 seconds. Option C uses inappropriate services. Option D (EC2 for weather models) doesn't provide HPC networking. Option E replaces numerical models with ML which isn't appropriate for operational weather forecasting.

question:
A company completed their AWS migration and now operates 150 accounts. They need to implement financial governance: accurate cost allocation to 20 business units, showback/chargeback reporting, budget management with enforcement, anomaly detection, and Reserved Instance/Savings Plan purchasing optimization. The finance team (non-technical) needs self-service access to cost data. Engineering teams need visibility into their own costs without seeing other teams' spending.

Which financial governance architecture provides comprehensive cost management?

options.A: AWS Cost Explorer for analysis. AWS Budgets per account. Cost allocation tags. Monthly manual reports from the finance team. Shared Reserved Instances across the organization.
options.B: AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) delivered to S3. Athena + QuickSight for custom cost dashboards (finance team self-service). Cost Categories mapping accounts and tags to business units. AWS Budgets with actions (notify, apply IAM policy) per business unit. AWS Cost Anomaly Detection per service and per account. Savings Plans purchased centrally with Cost Explorer recommendations. CUR data used by finance team in QuickSight without engineering involvement. Resource-level CUR enabling per-team cost visibility filtered by tags.
options.C: Third-party FinOps tool (CloudHealth/Apptio) for all cost management. Automated chargeback calculations. RI/SP optimization recommendations. Team-level dashboards.
options.D: AWS Organizations consolidated billing only. Management account admin distributes monthly PDF bills to business units. No programmatic cost analysis. RI purchases by individual accounts.
options.E: Cost Explorer with custom reports bookmarked per team. Trusted Advisor for optimization. Budgets alerts via email to finance. Savings Plans auto-renewal with same commitment.

explanation: This provides comprehensive financial governance using AWS-native tools: 1) CUR to S3 gives the most granular cost data (resource-level, hourly). 2) Athena + QuickSight enables the non-technical finance team to build and explore dashboards without engineering help (self-service BI on cost data). 3) Cost Categories automatically group costs by business unit using rules (account, tag, service) — no manual allocation. 4) Budgets with actions provide enforcement (can apply restrictive IAM policies when budget is exceeded). 5) Cost Anomaly Detection catches unexpected spikes per service and per account. 6) Centralized Savings Plans purchasing maximizes utilization across the organization (shared benefit). 7) QuickSight row-level security can show teams only their own costs. Option A is too basic (manual reports). Option C adds cost for a third-party tool when AWS-native covers the needs. Option D is minimal governance. Option E lacks the granular reporting and enforcement capabilities.

question:
A logistics company is building a supply chain visibility platform that must track 50 million packages across 200 countries in real-time. The system integrates with 500 carrier APIs (each with different data formats, update frequencies, and reliability levels), must reconcile conflicting status updates from multiple carriers, predict delivery times using ML models accounting for customs delays and weather, and provide a unified tracking interface to shippers and recipients. The platform must handle carrier API outages gracefully (cache last known status, retry, alert).

Which architecture handles the integration complexity and scale?

options.A: API Gateway for carrier webhooks. Lambda for format normalization. DynamoDB for package state. SageMaker for delivery predictions. CloudFront for tracking website. SQS for buffering carrier updates.
options.B: Amazon EventBridge with custom event bus for carrier events (each carrier mapped to an event source). EventBridge schema registry for format management. Step Functions for carrier API polling (for push-incompatible carriers) with circuit breaker pattern (exponential backoff on failures, cached last-known status in ElastiCache). Lambda for event normalization to canonical format. DynamoDB for package state machine (current status + full event history). Kinesis for streaming to ML prediction pipeline. SageMaker endpoint for ETA predictions. Conflict resolution Lambda comparing event timestamps and carrier priority rankings. AppSync for real-time tracking subscriptions.
options.C: Custom integration platform on ECS with adapter pattern (one adapter per carrier). Apache Kafka (MSK) for event streaming. Flink for stream processing and conflict resolution. PostgreSQL for package tracking state. Custom ML model on EC2 for predictions.
options.D: AWS B2B Data Interchange (formerly Data Exchange) for carrier integrations. Step Functions for orchestration. Aurora for package state. Comprehend for status text extraction. QuickSight for tracking dashboards.
options.E: MuleSoft (on EC2) for carrier API integration. DynamoDB for state. Lambda for processing. SageMaker for ML. API Gateway for customer-facing tracking.

explanation: This architecture handles the integration complexity at scale: 1) EventBridge custom event bus provides a unified event ingestion layer for all 500 carriers (webhooks publish directly, polled carriers feed through Step Functions). 2) Schema registry manages the 500 different formats. 3) Step Functions with circuit breaker pattern handles carrier API outages (backoff + cached status in ElastiCache during outages). 4) Lambda normalization converts all formats to a canonical event structure. 5) DynamoDB stores the package state machine with full event history (conflict resolution uses timestamps + carrier priority to determine authoritative status). 6) Kinesis feeds the ML pipeline for ETA prediction (aggregating events for feature engineering). 7) AppSync subscriptions push real-time updates to tracking consumers. This is operationally manageable using managed services. Option A is too simplified for 500 carrier integrations. Option C (Kafka + Flink + PostgreSQL) adds operational overhead. Option D (B2B Data Interchange) isn't designed for real-time tracking integrations. Option E (MuleSoft) adds licensing cost and operational complexity.

question:
A company has been running on AWS for 3 years. Their architecture has accumulated technical debt: unused resources costing $50K/month (orphaned EBS volumes, stopped instances with attached storage, unused Elastic IPs, idle load balancers), oversized resources costing an additional $80K/month in waste, security groups with overly permissive rules (0.0.0.0/0 on 200+ groups), and 500 unattached/unused IAM policies. The platform team (3 engineers) doesn't have capacity for manual cleanup while also delivering new features.

Which approach MOST effectively eliminates technical debt with minimal manual effort?

options.A: Hire contractors for a 3-month cleanup sprint. Manually review and clean each resource category. Document findings. Implement preventive measures after cleanup.
options.B: Implement automated cleanup pipelines: 1) AWS Config rules detecting each waste category + SSM Automation for remediation (delete orphaned EBS volumes older than 30 days with no recent snapshots, release unused EIPs, delete idle ALBs with zero targets for 14+ days). 2) Compute Optimizer recommendations auto-applied via Lambda for right-sizing (after team approval notification). 3) IAM Access Analyzer to identify unused policies → automated archival after 90 days of no use. 4) Security Hub + custom Config rule flagging 0.0.0.0/0 security groups → automated remediation replacing with VPC CIDR unless tagged as intentionally public. 5) Weekly Slack report showing cleanup actions taken and remaining debt. 6) Preventive SCPs and Config rules to prevent recurrence.
options.C: Use AWS Trusted Advisor for recommendations. Enable Cost Optimization Hub. Create Jira tickets for each finding. Assign to teams for manual resolution during sprint planning.
options.D: Delete all resources not tagged with a current project tag. Implement strict tagging going forward. Accept some disruption from incorrectly deleted resources.
options.E: Focus only on the highest-value items (right-sizing = $80K). Use Compute Optimizer and resize instances. Accept the $50K waste and security debt as lower priority.

explanation: Automated pipelines are the only approach that works for a 3-person team while delivering features: 1) Config rules + SSM Automation handles resource waste without human intervention (safe deletion rules: only orphaned volumes with no snapshots, only EIPs unattached for 30+ days, only ALBs with zero targets for 14+ days — conservative thresholds prevent accidental deletion). 2) Compute Optimizer with approval notification right-sizes without being dangerous (team approves before resize). 3) IAM Access Analyzer identifies truly unused policies programmatically (90-day observation period prevents premature removal). 4) Security group remediation replaces 0.0.0.0/0 with VPC CIDR unless intentionally public (safe default). 5) Preventive controls stop new debt from accumulating. 6) Weekly reports provide visibility without requiring action. Option A (contractors) is temporary with no sustainability. Option C (Jira tickets) creates work for the already-overloaded team. Option D (delete by tag) is reckless. Option E ignores most of the problem.

question:
A company is migrating their SAP S/4HANA environment to AWS. The environment includes: Production HANA database (6 TB in-memory), non-production systems (Quality, Development, Sandbox), SAP application servers (20 total across all systems), and integration with 30 third-party systems via SAP PI/PO middleware. They have a 36-hour maintenance window for the production cutover. The company wants to leverage AWS-specific capabilities post-migration (like backups, HA, and scaling) while maintaining SAP support.

Which migration approach ensures a successful SAP migration with SAP support maintained?

options.A: Use AWS Application Migration Service to rehost all SAP servers as-is. Maintain same OS and HANA versions. Configure Multi-AZ after migration. Use AWS Backup for SAP HANA.
options.B: Deploy SAP-certified EC2 instances (x2idn.metal for production HANA — provides 6 TB memory). Use SAP HANA System Replication (HSR) for migration: set up HSR from on-premises to AWS, replicate data, then perform takeover during the maintenance window (minimizes downtime). Non-production systems migrated using SAP backup/restore (faster, less risk-critical). SAP application servers rehosted on m5.4xlarge instances. AWS Launch Wizard for SAP used for HA configuration (automatic HANA HSR + Pacemaker cluster setup). AWS Backint agent for SAP HANA backups to S3. SAP PI/PO middleware migrated to EC2 with DNS-based cutover for third-party integrations.
options.C: Refactor SAP to run on Aurora PostgreSQL (eliminate HANA licensing costs). Containerize SAP application servers on EKS. Replace PI/PO with Amazon EventBridge for integration.
options.D: Use AWS Outposts to run SAP in the company's data center on AWS infrastructure. This maintains existing network connectivity to third-party systems and avoids migration complexity.
options.E: Migrate to SAP on Azure (Microsoft has deeper SAP partnership with specialized bare metal instances). Use Azure ExpressRoute for connectivity.

explanation: SAP HANA on AWS requires specific certified instance types and migration approaches to maintain SAP support: 1) x2idn.metal instances are SAP-certified for production HANA workloads with up to 6 TB memory. 2) HANA System Replication (HSR) is SAP's recommended migration approach — continuous replication followed by takeover minimizes the 36-hour window usage (actual downtime can be minutes for the takeover). 3) AWS Launch Wizard for SAP automates HA configuration following SAP best practices (HSR + Linux Pacemaker — required for SAP support). 4) AWS Backint agent is the SAP-certified backup solution for HANA on AWS (backs up directly to S3). 5) Non-production via backup/restore is faster and acceptable for non-critical systems. 6) DNS-based cutover for PI/PO maintains third-party integration without reconfiguration. Option A (MGN rehost) doesn't set up SAP-specific HA or use certified patterns. Option C (refactor to PostgreSQL) isn't supported for S/4HANA (requires HANA). Option D (Outposts) doesn't fully migrate. Option E suggests Azure which doesn't answer the AWS question.
